calendar_note: Islamic year 724 AH. Egyptian historian al-Umari records Musa's arrival in Cairo in July 1324 CE (Rajab 724 AH). Exact day not given.
main_actor: Mansa Musa I of the Mali Empire; Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad of the Mamluk Sultanate
description: Mansa Musa, emperor of Mali, arrived in Cairo in July 1324 on pilgrimage to Mecca. His caravan reportedly numbered 60,000 people including 12,000 enslaved attendants and 80-100 camels carrying gold. Al-Umari reported that his lavish gold disbursements in Cairo depressed the price of gold in Egypt for 10-12 years. First major European and Arab awareness of West African wealth; Mali placed on the 1375 Catalan Atlas.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the ~12,000 enslaved people in Musa's caravan are often omitted from popular accounts celebrating his wealth. The trans-Saharan slave trade moved ~9 million Africans north across the Sahara 700-1900 CE (Lovejoy 2011). Al-Umari's account is second-hand (he gathered it ~12 years after the visit).

sources.citation: al-Umari, Masalik al-absar fi mamalik al-amsar (c. 1338).
Levtzion, N. & Hopkins, J. F. P. (1981). Corpus of Early Arabic Sources for West African History. Cambridge UP.

name_original: Fall of Fatimid Caliphate — Saladin abolishes
calendar_note: Julian. Islamic: 7 Muharram 567 AH. Saladin ordered the Friday khutba read in the name of the Abbasid caliph on this Friday; the last Fatimid caliph al-Adid died a few days later.
main_actor: Salah al-Din (Saladin), Ayyubid vizier; al-Adid, last Fatimid caliph (died 13 September 1171)
description: Saladin formally ended the Ismaili Fatimid Caliphate by having Friday prayers recited in the name of the Sunni Abbasid caliph in Baghdad. The Fatimid al-Adid died days later. Saladin proclaimed himself Sultan, founding the Sunni Ayyubid dynasty of Egypt and Syria. End of ~260 years of Shia Fatimid rule (909-1171) based in Ifriqiya and then Cairo.
ethical_notes: The Fatimid era had been marked by considerable religious tolerance (Copts, Jews, and Sunnis in senior positions) and intellectual flourishing (al-Azhar founded 970 as a Shia seminary, later converted to Sunni). Saladin's takeover began the Sunnification of Egypt that continues today — Egyptian Shia became a tiny minority.

sources.citation: Ibn al-Athir, al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh (c. 1231).
Lev, Y. (1999). Saladin in Egypt. Brill.

calendar_note: Julian. Islamic: 27 Rajab 583 AH. The date was chosen to coincide with the traditional anniversary of Muhammad's Isra and Mi'raj (Night Journey to Jerusalem).
location_name: Jerusalem, Kingdom of Jerusalem (Crusader State) / retaken by Ayyubid Sultanate
main_actor: Salah al-Din (Saladin), Ayyubid Sultan; Balian of Ibelin (Crusader commander); Patriarch Heraclius
description: Saladin recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders 88 years after their 1099 massacre. Negotiated surrender under Balian of Ibelin's threat to raze the city — Christian inhabitants were allowed to ransom themselves (10 dinars for men, 5 for women, 1 for children), though thousands too poor were enslaved. No general massacre. The loss triggered the Third Crusade (1189-92). Note: 'فتح بيت المقدس / تحرير القدس' already in DB — possible overlap; flagging as possible duplicate.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'فتح بيت المقدس / تحرير القدس'. Saladin's relative clemency contrasts with the 1099 Crusader massacre (estimated 3,000-30,000 Jewish and Muslim inhabitants killed). His reputation for chivalry is partly a product of later European Romantic literature (Lessing, Scott).

sources.citation: Ibn Shaddad, al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya (c. 1228).

calendar_note: Islamic: 10 Rabi II 1302 AH.
main_actor: Muhammad Ahmad 'al-Mahdi'; Major-General Charles George Gordon (killed); Egyptian-Sudanese garrison
description: Mahdist forces stormed Khartoum after a 317-day siege, killing the Anglo-Egyptian governor Gordon and the garrison. The Mahdist State (1885-1898) was the first successful large-scale resistance to European colonialism in 19th-century Africa, ruling ~2.5 million km2. Khartoum's fall was a major shock to British public opinion, prompting W. E. Gladstone's political crisis and the 1898 reconquest (Battle of Omdurman).
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Mahdist state was brutal in its own right — forced Islamization, slave raids against non-Muslim southerners (Dinka, Nuer). British memory valorizes Gordon as martyr; Sudanese memory celebrates the Mahdi as liberator. Both framings are partial.

sources.citation: Holt, P. M. (1970). The Mahdist State in the Sudan, 1881-1898. Clarendon Press.

calendar_note: Julian calendar.
location_name: Kilwa Kisiwani, Sultanate of Kilwa (present-day Tanzania)
main_actor: Francisco de Almeida (first Portuguese Viceroy of India); Sultan Ibrahim ibn Sulayman (deposed)
description: Portuguese fleet under Francisco de Almeida seized Kilwa, one of the wealthiest Swahili city-states of the Indian Ocean trade network. Portuguese built the fort of Santiago, installed a puppet sultan, extracted tribute. Mombasa was sacked on 14 August 1505. Marked the violent insertion of European power into the Swahili-Arab trading system that had flourished for centuries. Note: DB has Swahili 'كيلوة كسوانى' — possibly YEAR precision; flagged as overlap.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'كيلوة كسوانى' (Kilwa Kisiwani). ETHICS: Portuguese records (Duarte Barbosa, Tome Pires) describe the Swahili civilization with respectful amazement, then proceeded to devastate it. The great stone cities of the coast (Kilwa, Mombasa, Malindi) decayed under Portuguese extraction; the Omani conquest in the 1690s re-established African sovereignty.

sources.citation: Duarte Barbosa, Livro (c. 1518).
Horton, M. & Middleton, J. (2000). The Swahili: The Social Landscape of a Mercantile Society. Blackwell.

name_original: Kingdom of Kongo Christian conversion — baptism of Nzinga a Nkuwu
calendar_note: Julian calendar. Portuguese sources (Rui de Pina) date the baptism to the feast of the Invention of the Holy Cross, 3 May 1491.
location_name: Mbanza Kongo (São Salvador), Kingdom of Kongo
main_actor: Manikongo Nzinga a Nkuwu (baptized as João I); Portuguese mission under Rui de Sousa
description: Manikongo Nzinga a Nkuwu of Kongo was baptized João I on 3 May 1491, making Kongo the first sub-Saharan African state to convert to Christianity via diplomatic choice rather than conquest. Relations with Portugal began with embassy exchanges and later became entangled in the slave trade. His son Afonso I (r. 1509-1543) was a literate Catholic theologian whose surviving letters complain about Portuguese slave trafficking in his kingdom.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Kongo Christianity became a distinctive syncretic tradition (cf. the Antonine movement of Beatriz Kimpa Vita, 1704-1706, a Kongolese woman who prophesied a Black Christ and was burned at the stake). The Portuguese alliance devolved into slaving predation that contributed to Kongo's 18th-century collapse.

sources.citation: Rui de Pina, Crónica del Rey Dom João II (1504).
Thornton, J. K. (1998). Africa and Africans in the Making of the Atlantic World, 2nd ed. Cambridge UP.

main_actor: Bernard Montgomery (British 8th Army); Erwin Rommel (Panzerarmee Afrika)
description: The Second Battle of El Alamein (23 October – 11 November 1942) was the turning point of the North African campaign. Montgomery's ~200,000 Allied troops with 1,000 tanks defeated Rommel's ~115,000 Axis troops with 550 tanks. Churchill called it 'the end of the beginning'. Led to the Axis collapse in North Africa by May 1943 and Allied landing in Sicily.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the North African campaign (1940-43) was fought on Egyptian, Libyan, and Tunisian soil with minimal regard for local populations. Egyptian civilians faced food shortages, the destruction of coastal villages, and post-war British rule continuing to 1954. Libya suffered extensive minefields still killing people decades later.

sources.citation: Barr, N. (2004). Pendulum of War: The Three Battles of El Alamein. Jonathan Cape.

location_name: Évian-les-Bains, France; applied in French Algeria
main_actor: Front de Libération Nationale (FLN); French government of Charles de Gaulle; Belkacem Krim (FLN signatory); Louis Joxe (France)
description: Évian Accords signed 18 March 1962 ending the Algerian War (1954-62). Ceasefire effective 19 March. Referendum in France (8 April 1962) and Algeria (1 July 1962) approved independence. Algeria proclaimed independence 5 July 1962, 132 years and one day after French conquest began (5 July 1830). The 8-year war had killed ~300,000-1.5 million Algerians and ~25,000 French soldiers; displaced ~2 million. Note: DB has 'ثورة التحرير الجزائرية / guerre d'algérie' — possibly refers to the entire war. Flagged but adding as signing event is distinct.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the French state waited until 1999 to legally recognize the Algerian War as a war (previously 'operations of maintaining order'). France has yet to fully acknowledge systematic torture, the use of napalm, or the 1961 Paris massacre of Algerian protesters. ~1 million pieds-noirs and ~200,000 harkis (Algerian auxiliaries) fled to France; ~60,000-150,000 harkis left behind were massacred.

sources.citation: Horne, A. (1977). A Savage War of Peace: Algeria 1954-1962. Macmillan.

main_actor: Kwame Nkrumah (first Prime Minister); Convention People's Party (CPP); Queen Elizabeth II (outgoing sovereign)
description: Ghana became the first sub-Saharan African nation to gain independence from European colonial rule on 6 March 1957. Nkrumah proclaimed 'Africa must unite' and Ghana became a Pan-Africanist beacon. Transition to republic (1 July 1960) with Nkrumah as president. Ghana's independence inspired decolonization across the continent — 17 African states became independent in 1960 alone ('Année de l'Afrique').
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Nkrumah's regime became authoritarian (1964 one-party state) and was overthrown by CIA-backed coup (24 February 1966). The post-independence trajectory in Ghana and elsewhere was shaped by Cold War interference, fragile institutions, and the persistence of colonial economic structures.

sources.citation: Birmingham, D. (1998). Kwame Nkrumah: The Father of African Nationalism. Ohio UP.

name_original: Abyssinia Crisis — Italian invasion of Ethiopia (1935)
main_actor: Benito Mussolini; Marshal Emilio De Bono then Pietro Badoglio; Emperor Haile Selassie I
description: Italy invaded Ethiopia on 3 October 1935 without declaration of war — three divisions crossed from Eritrea. War lasted until 5 May 1936 (occupation of Addis Ababa). Italy used chemical weapons (mustard gas) in violation of 1925 Geneva Protocol. ~275,000-760,000 Ethiopian casualties. League of Nations imposed ineffective sanctions; failure demonstrated the League's impotence and emboldened Axis expansion. Note: DB has 'seconda guerra italo-etiopica' — possibly YEAR precision; this adds the specific invasion date.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'seconda guerra italo-etiopica'. ETHICS: Italian atrocities during 1936-41 occupation (Yekatit 12 massacre of Addis Ababa 19-21 February 1937, ~30,000 killed in retaliation for attempt on Graziani) remain underacknowledged in Italian public memory. War criminals were never prosecuted.

sources.citation: Del Boca, A. (1979). La guerra d'Etiopia: l'ultima impresa del colonialismo. Longanesi.

name_original: Independence of the Democratic Republic of the Congo
main_actor: Patrice Lumumba (Prime Minister); Joseph Kasa-Vubu (President); King Baudouin I of Belgium
description: Independence of the Congo from Belgium on 30 June 1960. Baudouin's speech praised Leopold II, triggering Lumumba's unscheduled fiery response on Belgian colonial atrocities. Political crisis within days (army mutiny, Katangan secession under Tshombe 11 July, Kasai secession). Lumumba assassinated 17 January 1961 with Belgian and CIA involvement. Congo descended into chaos leading to Mobutu's 1965 coup and 32-year dictatorship.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Lumumba's independence-day speech — 'We have known the terrifying sufferings of those who were banished, day and night because we were nègres...' — remains one of the most important postcolonial documents. Belgian government apologized for Lumumba's murder only in 2002; King Philippe expressed 'regret' for colonial atrocities in 2020.

sources.citation: Zeilig, L. (2008). Lumumba: Africa's Lost Leader. Haus Publishing.

calendar_note: Julian calendar. In the Mexica xihuitl calendar: approximately 8 Wind in the year 1-Reed.
main_actor: Hernán Cortés; Huey Tlatoani Moctezuma II Xocoyotzin; Malintzin / La Malinche (interpreter); Tlaxcalteca allies
description: Hernán Cortés and ~500 Spanish soldiers, accompanied by Malintzin and ~6,000 Tlaxcalteca allies, entered Tenochtitlan on 8 November 1519 on the Iztapalapa causeway and were received by Moctezuma II. Cortés took Moctezuma hostage within days. The subsequent 2-year siege and conquest (1519-21) is one of the most consequential events in world history. Note: 'caída de tenochtitlan / tenōchtitlān īpoliuhyān' (fall) already in DB; this is the arrival/meeting, a distinct event.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Spanish sources (Cortés, Bernal Díaz) dominate the historiography; the native Florentine Codex (compiled 1545-90 by Sahagún and Nahua informants) offers a partial counter-narrative. Moctezuma's 'reception' as divine Quetzalcoatl returning is probably a post-conquest Spanish-imposed reading — pre-conquest Mexica sources don't support it.

sources.citation: Bernal Díaz del Castillo, Historia verdadera de la conquista de la Nueva España (c. 1568).
Sahagún, Códice Florentino, Book 12 (1577).
Restall, M. (2018). When Montezuma Met Cortés. Ecco/HarperCollins.

name_original: Night of Sorrows — La Noche Triste
calendar_note: Julian calendar. Night of 30 June - 1 July 1520.
main_actor: Hernán Cortés (retreat); Cuitláhuac (new Huey Tlatoani after Moctezuma's death); Spanish-Tlaxcalteca forces
description: Spanish and Tlaxcalteca forces attempted to flee Tenochtitlan during the night of 30 June – 1 July 1520 after the Mexica uprising triggered by the Templo Mayor massacre (Pedro de Alvarado, 20 May 1520). ~600-800 Spanish and ~2,000 Tlaxcalteca killed attempting to cross the Tacuba causeway; most Spanish treasure lost. Cortés regrouped in Tlaxcala, returned with smallpox-aided siege force, capturing Tenochtitlan 13 August 1521.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: 'La Noche Triste' is a Spanish framing. The Mexica call it the night they nearly expelled the invaders. Moctezuma II died during or shortly after the uprising (Spanish sources say stoned by his own people; native sources suggest Spanish murdered him). The Mexica won the battle but lost the war because of smallpox (arrived with Narváez's expedition) killing ~40% of the city's population.

sources.citation: Thomas, H. (1993). Conquest: Montezuma, Cortés, and the Fall of Old Mexico. Simon & Schuster.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; Spanish sources give the evening of 26 July 1533.
main_actor: Francisco Pizarro; Sapa Inca Atahualpa (garroted); Friar Vicente de Valverde; Diego de Almagro
description: Sapa Inca Atahualpa, having paid a room of gold and two of silver as ransom (~6 tonnes of gold, ~12 tonnes of silver — the largest ransom ever paid), was garroted by the Spanish on 26 July 1533 after a summary trial. Pizarro installed the puppet Inca Túpac Huallpa (poisoned within months), then Manco Inca. Atahualpa's execution is foundational to Latin American historical memory of conquista.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Atahualpa was convicted on fabricated charges (idolatry, polygamy, treason against the Spanish Crown — to whom he owed no allegiance). Spanish justification mixed theological and legal gloss over what was unambiguously judicial murder for political ends. Indigenous oral tradition (recorded by Garcilaso de la Vega, Guaman Poma) preserves memorial consciousness of the event.

sources.citation: Guaman Poma de Ayala, El primer nueva corónica y buen gobierno (c. 1615).
Hemming, J. (1970). The Conquest of the Incas. Macmillan.

name_original: Treaty of Paris 1783 — end of American Revolution
location_name: Hôtel d'York, Paris, Kingdom of France
main_actor: Benjamin Franklin, John Adams, John Jay (US); David Hartley (Britain)
description: Treaty of Paris signed 3 September 1783 ending the American Revolutionary War (1775-1783). Britain recognized US independence and ceded territory west to the Mississippi (to Spanish territory), north to the Great Lakes, south to the 31st parallel. Fishing rights off Newfoundland preserved for US. Loyalists' property to be 'earnestly recommended' for restoration (largely ignored; ~60,000 loyalists fled). Inaugurated the United States as a recognized sovereign nation.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the treaty ignored the ~100,000+ Indigenous peoples whose lands were transferred to US jurisdiction. The US proceeded with aggressive dispossession (Northwest Indian War 1785-95, Creek War 1813-14, Indian Removal Act 1830). Loyalist Black people — freed by British proclamations — were evacuated to Nova Scotia and Sierra Leone; ~3,000 'book of negroes' freedpeople.

sources.citation: Middlekauff, R. (1982). The Glorious Cause: The American Revolution, 1763-1789. Oxford UP.

name_original: Simón Bolívar enters Caracas — Second Venezuelan Republic
main_actor: Simón Bolívar; Captain-General Domingo de Monteverde (royalist, defeated)
description: After the Admirable Campaign (May-August 1813), Simón Bolívar entered Caracas on 6 August 1813 as 'Libertador' and founded the Second Republic of Venezuela. The republic collapsed the following year under José Tomás Boves's royalist llaneros. Bolívar went into exile in Jamaica, wrote the Carta de Jamaica (6 September 1815), returned for the decisive independence campaign.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Bolívar's 'Guerra a Muerte' decree (15 June 1813) authorized killing all Spanish-born civilians — a rhetorical and actual racialization of the war. The wars of independence killed an estimated 10-20% of Venezuela's population (1810-23). Bolívar's later relationship with enslaved Black combatants (many fought on both sides) was ambivalent; general emancipation came only in 1854.

sources.citation: Lynch, J. (2006). Simón Bolívar: A Life. Yale UP.

location_name: Boyacá bridge, Viceroyalty of New Granada
main_actor: Simón Bolívar and Francisco de Paula Santander (patriots); Colonel José María Barreiro (royalist, captured)
description: Bolívar's patriot army, having crossed the Andes in an unprecedented military feat, defeated the royalist army at Boyacá bridge in a ~2-hour battle. ~100 royalist killed, 1,600 captured including Barreiro; ~13 patriots killed. Liberated Santa Fe de Bogotá (10 August 1819) and enabled the creation of Gran Colombia (17 December 1819, Angostura) comprising present-day Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Panama, and parts of Peru, Brazil, and Guyana.
ethical_notes: Colombia's national day (20 July 1810, Grito de Independencia) and Boyacá (7 August 1819) mark independence declaration and military victory respectively. Gran Colombia dissolved in 1830-31, the year of Bolívar's death, demonstrating the fragility of his pan-Andean project.

sources.citation: Bushnell, D. (1993). The Making of Modern Colombia. California UP.

location_name: Ipiranga creek, São Paulo, Kingdom of Brazil
main_actor: Pedro I of Brazil (crown prince of Portugal); José Bonifácio de Andrada e Silva (patriarch of independence)
description: Pedro I declared Brazilian independence from Portugal with the cry 'Independência ou morte!' on the banks of the Ipiranga creek, 7 September 1822. Crowned Emperor 1 December 1822. Brazil transitioned from Portuguese colony (since 1500) to independent empire with the existing ruling dynasty — an unusual monarchical path to independence without republican rupture. Empire lasted until 1889. Note: DB does not appear to have this event; adding as new.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Brazilian independence preserved slavery — which lasted until 13 May 1888 (Lei Áurea), the longest slavery regime in the Americas. ~5 million Africans were forcibly transported to Brazil across the Atlantic slave trade — more than any other destination. The Empire's abolitionist turn accelerated its 1889 collapse.

sources.citation: Schultz, K. (2001). Tropical Versailles: Empire, Monarchy, and the Portuguese Royal Court in Rio de Janeiro, 1808-1821. Routledge.

name_original: Texas Revolution — Battle of San Jacinto
location_name: San Jacinto, Republic of Mexico (Coahuila y Texas)
main_actor: Sam Houston (Texian); General Antonio López de Santa Anna (Mexican, captured)
description: Texian army under Sam Houston defeated Santa Anna's Mexican army at San Jacinto in an 18-minute battle. 630 Mexicans killed, 730 captured including Santa Anna. Houston lost 9 men. Santa Anna signed the Treaties of Velasco (14 May 1836) recognizing Texian independence (repudiated by the Mexican government, setting up the 1846 Mexican-American War). Republic of Texas existed 1836-1845 until US annexation.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Texas secession was largely motivated by slavery — 1829 Mexican abolition had made slave-owning Anglo colonists uneasy. Texas independence constitution reinstated slavery. Annexation by the US (1845) triggered the Mexican-American War and the seizure of ~55% of Mexican territory. Traditional Mexican memory calls this dismemberment.

sources.citation: Brands, H. W. (2004). Lone Star Nation: How a Ragged Army of Volunteers Won the Battle for Texas Independence. Anchor.

location_name: Ford's Theatre, Washington, D.C., United States
main_actor: John Wilkes Booth (assassin); President Abraham Lincoln (shot 14 April, died 07:22 15 April)
description: President Abraham Lincoln was shot at Ford's Theatre by actor John Wilkes Booth during a performance of 'Our American Cousin' at approximately 22:15 on 14 April 1865 — five days after Lee's surrender at Appomattox. Lincoln died 07:22 15 April 1865 at the Petersen House across the street. First presidential assassination in US history. Vice President Andrew Johnson succeeded. Booth was shot dead in a Virginia tobacco barn 26 April.
ethical_notes: Booth's motive was Confederate vengeance (his co-conspirators also attacked Secretary Seward and planned to kill Johnson). Johnson's lenient Reconstruction reversed many Black political gains, enabling the 'Redeemer' counterrevolution and the system of Jim Crow laws lasting to 1965.

sources.citation: Swanson, J. L. (2006). Manhunt: The 12-Day Chase for Lincoln's Killer. William Morrow.

location_name: New York Stock Exchange, New York City
main_actor: NYSE traders; Federal Reserve (Young, Hoover)
description: Black Thursday — panic selling on the NYSE on 24 October 1929. Further collapses on Black Monday (28 October, -13%) and Black Tuesday (29 October, -12%). DJIA lost ~25% in two days, ~89% by July 1932 trough. Triggered the Great Depression (1929-39), banking crises (1930-33), and the New Deal (1933-). Global propagation caused mass unemployment (US ~25%, Germany 30%) and the political conditions for fascist rise in Europe. Note: DB has 'wall street crash / great depression onset' — possibly YEAR precision; this is the specific day.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — may duplicate 'wall street crash / great depression onset'. ETHICS: the Depression disproportionately harmed already-marginalized communities. Dust Bowl migration (Okies) coincided with mass deportation of Mexican Americans (~400,000-2 million, many US citizens). African-American unemployment ran ~50%. Indigenous poverty deepened.

sources.citation: Galbraith, J. K. (1954). The Great Crash, 1929. Houghton Mifflin.

location_name: Little Bighorn River, Montana Territory, United States
main_actor: Lakota, Northern Cheyenne, Arapaho warriors under Sitting Bull, Crazy Horse; Lt Col George Armstrong Custer (killed)
description: Combined Lakota-Cheyenne-Arapaho force of ~1,500-2,500 warriors defeated the US 7th Cavalry at the Little Bighorn River. Custer and ~268 of his men were killed; Native casualties ~36-136. Greatest Indigenous military victory over US forces. US reprisal campaigns (1876-77) forced Lakota surrender; Crazy Horse killed in custody 1877; Sitting Bull fled to Canada, surrendered 1881, killed 1890 preceding the Wounded Knee massacre.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Lakota call this the Battle of the Greasy Grass. The battle was defensive — US forces entered unceded Lakota territory (1868 Fort Laramie Treaty) to force reservation compliance after gold was discovered in the Black Hills (Hé Sápa, sacred to Lakota). The US Supreme Court in United States v. Sioux Nation (1980) found the Black Hills had been unlawfully seized; Lakota have refused the ~$1 billion compensation, demanding land back.

sources.citation: Donovan, J. (2008). A Terrible Glory: Custer and the Little Bighorn. Little, Brown.

main_actor: Jean-Jacques Dessalines; Boisrond-Tonnerre (secretary); veterans of the Haitian Revolution
description: Dessalines proclaimed Haiti's independence from France on 1 January 1804 at Gonaïves, renaming Saint-Domingue to its Taíno name 'Ayiti'. The only successful large-scale slave revolt in the modern world. Haiti was the second independent republic in the Americas (after US) and the first Black-led independent nation. Dessalines was crowned Emperor 8 October 1804, assassinated 17 October 1806. Note: DB has 'haitian revolution / revolisyon ayisyen' — possibly YEAR or range precision; this adds specific day of independence declaration.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: France imposed a ruinous 150-million-franc indemnity on Haiti in 1825 (under threat of re-conquest) as 'compensation' for lost slaves — a debt that crippled Haitian development and was only paid off in 1947. Western powers mostly refused to recognize Haiti (US until 1862). Dessalines ordered the killings of 3,000-5,000 remaining white French in early 1804 — the 'other' side of the event often omitted.

sources.citation: Dubois, L. (2004). Avengers of the New World: The Story of the Haitian Revolution. Harvard UP.

name_original: Pedro Álvares Cabral lands in Brazil
calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 1 May 1500.
location_name: Monte Pascoal, Bahia, South America (Tupiniquim territory)
main_actor: Pedro Álvares Cabral (Portuguese fleet commander); Tupiniquim people; Pero Vaz de Caminha (scribe)
description: Portuguese fleet commanded by Pedro Álvares Cabral sighted Monte Pascoal on 22 April 1500 and anchored, making contact with Tupiniquim inhabitants. Pero Vaz de Caminha's letter to King Manuel I (1 May 1500) is the 'birth certificate' of Brazil. Cabral sailed on to India. Portuguese settlement followed from 1532 (Martim Afonso de Sousa).
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Cabral was part of a squadron bound for Calicut — the westward detour was either intentional (Portuguese may have had prior knowledge of Brazil from João Coelho's hypothetical earlier voyage or from Duarte Pacheco Pereira's 1498 mission) or accidental. Caminha's letter describes the Tupiniquim with ethnographic curiosity; within decades, disease, enslavement, and violence devastated coastal Tupian populations.

sources.citation: Caminha, P. V. (1500). Carta a el-Rei D. Manuel sobre o achamento do Brasil.
Greenlee, W. B. (1938). The Voyage of Pedro Álvares Cabral to Brazil and India. Hakluyt Society.

calendar_note: Gregorian. In the Japanese lunar calendar: 15th day, 9th month of Keichō 5 (慶長五年九月十五日).
main_actor: Tokugawa Ieyasu (Eastern Army); Ishida Mitsunari (Western Army, defeated); Kobayakawa Hideaki (defector)
description: Decisive battle of the Sengoku period. ~88,000 Eastern Army troops defeated ~82,000 Western forces when Kobayakawa Hideaki defected to Ieyasu's side mid-afternoon. Ended ~150 years of civil war. Three years later (1603) Ieyasu was appointed shōgun, founding the Tokugawa Bakufu, which ruled until 1868.
ethical_notes: The Tokugawa regime that followed imposed strict social hierarchy (shi-nō-kō-shō), closed the country to most foreign contact (sakoku 1635-1853), and suppressed Christianity through mass executions (Shimabara Rebellion 1637-38, ~37,000 killed).

sources.citation: Bryant, A. (1995). Sekigahara 1600. Osprey.
Tokugawa Jikki 徳川実紀 (compiled 1809-1849).

name_original: Commodore Perry arrives at Uraga Bay
location_name: Uraga Bay, Edo Bay, Tokugawa Japan
main_actor: Commodore Matthew C. Perry, USN; Tokugawa bakufu officials; Emperor Kōmei (observer)
description: US Commodore Perry's 'Black Ship' squadron (four steam frigates) entered Uraga Bay on 8 July 1853, demanding Japan open to trade. The show of force, followed by Perry's 1854 return, led to the Convention of Kanagawa (31 March 1854) ending 220 years of sakoku. Triggered the internal crisis that culminated in the Meiji Restoration (1868).
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Perry's mission was gunboat diplomacy. His letter from President Fillmore threatened consequences if refused. The 'unequal treaties' imposed on Japan (1854-58) — extraterritoriality, fixed tariffs — shaped Meiji determination to achieve legal equality, which partly motivated Japan's own later imperialist expansion.

sources.citation: Perry, M. C. (1856). Narrative of the Expedition of an American Squadron to the China Seas and Japan. Washington.

name_original: Meiji Restoration — Imperial Oath (Gokajō no Goseimon)
calendar_note: Gregorian. Japanese lunar calendar: 14th day, 3rd month of Keiō 4 / Meiji 1 (慶応四年三月十四日).
main_actor: Emperor Meiji (Mutsuhito); Iwakura Tomomi, Kido Takayoshi, Ōkubo Toshimichi (Meiji oligarchs)
description: The Charter Oath (Gokajō no Goseimon) of 6 April 1868 (lunar 14 March Keiō 4), promulgated the following day, declared five principles: deliberative assemblies, universal participation in national affairs, freedom of occupation, abandonment of 'evil customs', and adoption of knowledge from abroad. Formally inaugurated the Meiji era. Over the next 30 years, Japan transformed into an industrial power with a conscript army, constitutional government (1889), and imperial ambitions.
ethical_notes: The Meiji modernization had massive costs: samurai class abolished (many impoverished), 1870s-80s peasant revolts brutally suppressed, Ainu and Ryūkyūan peoples subjected to forced assimilation (Hokkaidō colonization from 1869; Ryūkyū Kingdom annexed 1879). Japan's imperial expansion (Taiwan 1895, Korea 1910, Manchuria 1931) flowed directly from Meiji institutions.

sources.citation: Jansen, M. B. (2000). The Making of Modern Japan. Harvard UP.

location_name: Nanchang, Jiangxi Province, Republic of China
main_actor: Zhou Enlai, Zhu De, He Long, Ye Ting, Liu Bocheng (CCP); Wang Jingwei's KMT Left government
description: On 1 August 1927, Chinese Communist Party forces under Zhou Enlai and Zhu De seized Nanchang in revolt against the Kuomintang after Chiang Kai-shek's April 1927 Shanghai Massacre of Communists. Though the uprising failed within days and the rebels retreated, the date is commemorated as the founding day of the People's Liberation Army (now celebrated as PLA Day).
ethical_notes: Official PRC narrative presents Nanchang as a clean founding moment for the PLA; the actual force was a coalition of KMT left-wing and CCP units that rapidly disintegrated. Zhu De's later Jinggang Mountain force (1928) was the actual nucleus of the Red Army.

sources.citation: van de Ven, H. (1991). From Friend to Comrade: The Founding of the Chinese Communist Party. California UP.

calendar_note: Gregorian. In the Chinese lunar calendar: 19th day, 8th month of Xuantong 3. Commemorated as 'Double Ten' (10/10) by the Republic of China.
main_actor: New Army mutineers led by Xiong Bingkun; Tongmenghui (Sun Yat-sen's league); Huang Xing; Li Yuanhong (reluctant figurehead)
description: Mutiny of New Army units at Wuchang on 10 October 1911 triggered province-after-province declarations of independence from the Qing. Within four months, 14 of 18 provinces had seceded. Last emperor Puyi abdicated 12 February 1912. Sun Yat-sen proclaimed the Republic of China (1 January 1912), ending ~2,100 years of imperial rule that began with Qin Shi Huang (221 BCE).
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Revolution's Han-nationalist rhetoric ('expel the Manchus') led to anti-Manchu pogroms in Xi'an, Wuchang, Nanjing, and elsewhere (thousands killed). Manchu populations were subsequently subject to systematic discrimination. The transition ushered in the Warlord Era (1916-28) with massive civil-war casualties.

sources.citation: Esherick, J. (1976). Reform and Revolution in China: The 1911 Revolution in Hunan and Hubei. California UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 1 May 1526. In the Islamic calendar: 8 Rajab 932 AH.
main_actor: Zahir al-Din Muhammad Babur (Timurid, future first Mughal emperor); Sultan Ibrahim Lodi (Delhi, killed in battle)
description: Babur's ~15,000-strong Timurid force, equipped with matchlock guns and field artillery (first large-scale use in northern India), defeated Ibrahim Lodi's ~100,000-strong army including ~100 war elephants. Lodi was killed on the field. Founded the Mughal Empire, which would rule most of the Indian subcontinent until 1857.
ethical_notes: Babur's autobiography (Baburnama) is a remarkable primary source, candid about his own ethnic and religious prejudices. The Mughal state that followed was itself religiously plural in practice under Akbar (r. 1556-1605) but became more sectarian under Aurangzeb (r. 1658-1707).

sources.citation: Babur, Baburnama (translated Thackston 2002, Modern Library).
Dale, S. F. (2004). The Garden of the Eight Paradises: Babur and the Culture of Empire. Brill.

calendar_note: Julian calendar. Islamic: 2 Muharram 964 AH.
main_actor: Bairam Khan (Mughal regent) and Emperor Akbar (aged 13); Hemu (Hindu general of Adil Shah Suri, killed)
description: Mughal forces under Bairam Khan defeated Hemu, general and de-facto ruler of the Sur Empire who had proclaimed himself Emperor Vikramaditya. Hemu was struck by a stray arrow, captured, and executed. The victory consolidated Akbar's throne after his father Humayun's death earlier that year and reopened the path to Akbar's expansive reign (1556-1605).
ethical_notes: Hemu's brief elevation to Emperor Vikramaditya is an often-overlooked episode of a Hindu claimant to imperial sovereignty in Delhi between Mughal reigns.

sources.citation: Richards, J. F. (1993). The Mughal Empire. Cambridge UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar. Portuguese sources (Brás de Albuquerque, Comentários) date the final assault to St Bartholomew's Day, 24 August 1511.
main_actor: Afonso de Albuquerque (Portuguese Viceroy of India); Sultan Mahmud Shah of Malacca (fled)
description: Portuguese conquest of Malacca under Afonso de Albuquerque, ending the Malacca Sultanate. ~1,200 Portuguese and ~300 Indian soldiers took the city after a month of fighting. Malacca had been the commercial capital of Southeast Asia linking China, India, and the Spice Islands. Portuguese control (1511-1641) inaugurated European naval dominance of the Strait of Malacca, one of the world's most vital maritime chokepoints.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Albuquerque's conquest involved massacres and the enslavement of survivors; the former Sultan's palace was burned. Malay historiography (Sulalat al-Salatin / Sejarah Melayu) remembers the event as catastrophe. Portuguese control devastated the Muslim trading networks of SE Asia and set the template for European armed trading monopoly.

sources.citation: Commentarios do Grande Afonso Dalboquerque, ed. Bulhão Pato (1884).
Subrahmanyam, S. (2012). The Portuguese Empire in Asia 1500-1700. Wiley-Blackwell.

calendar_note: Gregorian calendar (Austria had adopted it in 1584); Julian equivalent 2 September 1683.
location_name: Kahlenberg heights, outside Vienna, Holy Roman Empire
main_actor: King Jan III Sobieski of Poland-Lithuania; Emperor Leopold I (fled earlier); Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa Pasha (defeated, executed December 1683 by sultan's order)
description: Ottoman siege of Vienna broken on 12 September 1683 by a Polish-German-Austrian relief army under Jan Sobieski. The Winged Hussar charge (~18,000 cavalry, largest in history) broke the Ottoman line. Kara Mustafa's ~150,000-strong army lost ~15,000-20,000 killed. Turned the Ottoman tide in Europe; subsequent Great Turkish War (1683-99) ended with the Treaty of Karlowitz (1699) and Ottoman loss of Hungary.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Vienna 1683 is routinely invoked by European far-right narratives as a 'civilizational' event. Historically, the Habsburg-Ottoman conflict was a state-versus-state dynastic struggle with Christian Hungarians (Thököly's kurucs) fighting alongside the Ottomans, and Tatar/Moldavian auxiliaries on the Ottoman side fighting Polish co-religionists. The date (11-12 September) coincidentally echoes in later '9/11' symbolic mythmaking.

sources.citation: Wheatcroft, A. (2008). The Enemy at the Gate: Habsburgs, Ottomans, and the Battle for Europe. Basic Books.

location_name: Aboard HMS Cornwallis, Nanjing, Qing Empire
main_actor: Henry Pottinger (British plenipotentiary); Qiying and Yilibu (Qing plenipotentiaries); Emperor Daoguang
description: Treaty ending the First Opium War. Qing China ceded Hong Kong Island to Britain in perpetuity, opened five treaty ports (Guangzhou, Xiamen, Fuzhou, Ningbo, Shanghai), paid $21 million silver indemnity, abolished the Cohong monopoly, and fixed tariffs externally. First of the 'unequal treaties'. Opened the Century of Humiliation (百年国耻) in Chinese collective memory.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the war was fought to force China to accept British opium (grown in India) as a medium to balance the tea trade. The treaty inaugurated a century of forced concessions to European, American, and Japanese powers. Hong Kong returned to China only in 1997.

sources.citation: Lovell, J. (2011). The Opium War: Drugs, Dreams and the Making of Modern China. Picador.

calendar_note: Julian. Japanese lunar: 7th day, 2nd month of Juei 3.
main_actor: Minamoto no Yoshitsune and Minamoto no Noriyori (Genji); Taira no Tadanori and Taira no Atsumori (Heike, killed)
description: Minamoto victory over the Taira at the coastal fortress of Ichinotani. Yoshitsune's famous downhill cavalry charge from Hiyodori Pass broke the Taira defenses. The Taira fled by sea; the young warrior Atsumori, killed by Kumagai Naozane, became a figure of enduring literary and Noh tragedy. Major step toward the Minamoto victory at Dan-no-ura (1185, already in DB).
ethical_notes: The Heike Monogatari (c. 1240) is the chief narrative source — a literary epic composed as Buddhist meditation on impermanence, not factual reportage. Historical reconstruction relies also on the Azuma Kagami shogunal chronicle.

sources.citation: Heike Monogatari (c. 1240), trans. Royall Tyler (2012).

name_original: Assassination of Prime Minister Itō Hirobumi
location_name: Harbin railway station, Russian Concession in Qing Manchuria
main_actor: An Jung-geun / 안중근 (Korean independence activist); Itō Hirobumi (killed, former Japanese Resident-General of Korea)
description: Korean independence activist An Jung-geun shot Itō Hirobumi, four-time Prime Minister of Japan and architect of the 1905 Japanese protectorate over Korea, at Harbin station on 26 October 1909. An was tried, convicted, and executed by Japanese authorities 26 March 1910. The assassination accelerated Japanese annexation of Korea (22 August 1910). An Jung-geun is revered as a national hero in both Koreas.
ethical_notes: An's 'Treatise on Peace in the East' (동양평화론), written in Japanese prison, proposed a federal East Asian union of Korea, China, and Japan as alternative to imperial domination. The work was unfinished at his execution. Japanese textbooks routinely minimize An; South and North Korean memory celebrate him as martyr.

sources.citation: Eckert, C. et al. (1990). Korea Old and New: A History. Ilchokak/Harvard UP.

calendar_note: Gregorian calendar. Japanese lunar: 4th day, 10th month of Hōei 4.
location_name: Nankai Trough, Pacific coast of Japan
main_actor: Nankai Trough megathrust (Mw ~8.6-8.9)
description: Great Hōei earthquake, one of the largest in Japanese history, rupturing ~700 km of the Nankai Trough. Tsunami waves up to 10 m killed thousands along the Pacific coast. Estimated total deaths 5,000-20,000. Seven weeks later (16 December 1707), Mount Fuji erupted for the final time in its recorded history (Hōei eruption), blanketing Edo in ash.
ethical_notes: Japanese earthquake history is exceptionally well-documented thanks to continuous record-keeping by temples and government. Such 'paired' tectonic events (earthquake + volcanic response) inform modern hazard assessment for Tokyo, where a recurrence would be catastrophic.

sources.citation: Ishibashi, K. (2004). Status of historical seismology in Japan. Annals of Geophysics 47(2-3).

calendar_note: Sanskrit inscriptions give regnal years of Suryavarman II (r. 1113 – c. 1150). Temple completion and posthumous dedication approximately 1150 CE. Precise day not recorded.
main_actor: King Suryavarman II of the Khmer Empire
description: Completion and dedication of Angkor Wat, the largest religious monument in the world by area (~162 hectares). Originally dedicated to Vishnu. Construction took ~30 years under Suryavarman II (r. 1113-c.1150). The temple was later transformed into a Theravada Buddhist site (from 13th century). Remains continuously in religious use for ~870 years.
ethical_notes: Khmer inscriptions in Sanskrit and Old Khmer provide regnal contexts, but the specific dedication ceremony is not directly dated. The French colonial 're-discovery' narrative of Angkor (Mouhot 1860) erased continuous Cambodian religious use.

sources.citation: Chandler, D. (2008). A History of Cambodia, 4th ed. Westview.

name_original: Fall of Vijayanagara — Battle of Talikota
calendar_note: Julian calendar; 23 January 1565 per some sources. Islamic: 25 Jumada II 972 AH. Note: DB has Kannada 'ತಾಳಿಕೋಟೆ ಯುದ್ಧ' — possibly YEAR precision; flagged as possible overlap. Keeping this as NEW addition since DB entry may lack day/month.
main_actor: Deccan Sultanates coalition (Ahmadnagar, Bijapur, Golconda, Bidar); Aliya Rama Raya of Vijayanagara (killed in battle)
description: Alliance of four Deccan Sultanates decisively defeated the Vijayanagara Empire at Talikota. Rama Raya was captured and beheaded. The victorious armies sacked Vijayanagara city (Hampi) for six months, reducing the continent's largest medieval city to ruins. Effectively ended the Vijayanagara Empire, though successor states lingered until 1646.
ethical_notes: SKIP if 'ತಾಳಿಕೋಟೆ ಯುದ್ಧ' already present — flagged in README. ETHICS: Hampi's destruction involved mass killings and enslavement of Hindu inhabitants; contemporary Portuguese observer Fernão Nunes wrote vivid accounts. The battle is often retrospectively framed as Hindu-Muslim civilizational clash, but Vijayanagara itself employed Muslim commanders and the Sultanates had Hindu vassals — the conflict was state-versus-state.

sources.citation: Eaton, R. M. (2005). A Social History of the Deccan, 1300-1761. Cambridge UP.

main_actor: Itō Hirobumi (Japan); Li Hongzhang (Qing China, wounded in assassination attempt 24 March 1895)
description: Treaty ending the First Sino-Japanese War (1894-95). Qing China recognized Korean independence (ending tributary relationship), ceded Taiwan, the Pescadores, and the Liaodong Peninsula (returned under Triple Intervention 1895), paid a 200-million-tael indemnity, and opened four additional treaty ports. Japan's first imperialist acquisition; marked the transition of regional primacy from China to Japan.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the treaty ignored Taiwanese and Korean voices. Taiwan's brief Republic of Formosa (May-October 1895) attempted resistance and was crushed by Japanese invasion. ~14,000 Taiwanese killed in the conquest. Korea's nominal independence became a Japanese sphere (1897 Korean Empire), protectorate (1905), annexation (1910).

sources.citation: Paine, S. C. M. (2003). The Sino-Japanese War of 1894-1895. Cambridge UP.

location_name: Tsushima Strait, between Korea and Japan
main_actor: Admiral Tōgō Heihachirō (Japan); Admiral Zinovy Rozhestvensky (Russia, captured)
description: On 27-28 May 1905, the Imperial Japanese Navy annihilated the Russian Baltic Fleet which had sailed 18,000 nautical miles to reach East Asia. Of 38 Russian warships, 21 were sunk and 7 captured. ~4,380 Russians killed, ~5,900 captured. Japanese losses 117 killed. First modern defeat of a European great power by a non-European power. Led to Treaty of Portsmouth (5 September 1905) ending the Russo-Japanese War.
ethical_notes: Tsushima had global anticolonial resonance — celebrated by Indian, Egyptian, Vietnamese, and African nationalists as proof that European power was not invincible. The Japanese victory also cemented Japan's expansionist path and the Korean protectorate.

sources.citation: Westwood, J. N. (1986). Russia Against Japan, 1904-05. SUNY Press.

main_actor: Taliban forces (Mullah Omar); Ahmad Shah Massoud (Northern Alliance, retreated); former President Mohammad Najibullah (murdered, body hung in Kabul)
description: Taliban forces entered Kabul on 26-27 September 1996, ending the fractured post-Soviet Mujahideen government. Former Communist president Najibullah, sheltered in the UN compound, was abducted, mutilated, and hung publicly. Taliban imposed strict Deobandi-inflected social code — women barred from education and most work, public executions restored, music and television banned. Regime ruled 1996-2001 until US invasion; returned 2021.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Taliban's rise owed much to Pakistani ISI support, Saudi financing, and US acquiescence (viewing them as anti-Iranian and anti-Russian). Civilian costs — particularly for women, Hazaras, and minorities — were catastrophic. Mazar-i-Sharif massacre (1998) killed thousands of Hazaras.

sources.citation: Rashid, A. (2001). Taliban: Islam, Oil and the New Great Game in Central Asia. I. B. Tauris.

location_name: Ramna Race Course, Dhaka (People's Republic of Bangladesh)
main_actor: Lt General A. A. K. Niazi (Pakistan, surrendered); Lt General Jagjit Singh Aurora (India); Mukti Bahini (Bangladesh liberation fighters)
description: Lt General Niazi surrendered 93,000 Pakistani troops to joint Indian-Mukti Bahini command at Dhaka's Ramna Race Course on 16 December 1971, ending the Bangladesh Liberation War. Birangona (freedom fighter) and Shaheed Minar narratives center Bengali resistance. The nine-month Pakistani military campaign (Operation Searchlight, March-December 1971) killed between 300,000 and 3 million Bengalis and raped ~200,000-400,000 women — a genocide acknowledged by US Consul General Archer Blood's cabled dissent (April 1971).
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Pakistani historiography continues to minimize or deny the 1971 genocide. Bangladesh's own postwar period saw political violence, Bihari minority displacement, and contested truth-and-reconciliation. The casualty range (300K-3M) reflects ongoing scholarly dispute.

sources.citation: Bass, G. J. (2013). The Blood Telegram: Nixon, Kissinger, and a Forgotten Genocide. Knopf.

name_original: Jihad of Usman dan Fodio — declaration of Sokoto Caliphate
calendar_note: Gregorian calendar. Islamic: 10 Dhu al-Qada 1218 AH. Usman dan Fodio's hijra from Degel to Gudu on 21 February 1804.
main_actor: Shehu Usman dan Fodio (Fulani Muslim scholar); Muhammad Bello (his son, successor); Sarkin Gobir Yunfa (Hausa king, defeated)
description: Usman dan Fodio declared jihad and withdrew (hijra) from Degel to Gudu on 21 February 1804, escaping the Gobir king Yunfa who had outlawed his community. Over the following 5 years, Fulani and allied Hausa forces overthrew the Hausa city-states and established the Sokoto Caliphate (capital: Sokoto, founded 1809). At its peak the caliphate covered ~500,000 km² with ~10 million inhabitants — one of the largest African polities of the 19th century until British conquest (1903). Note: DB has 'جهاد عثمان دان فوديو' — likely YEAR precision; this adds the specific hijra day.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'جهاد عثمان دان فوديو'. ETHICS: the jihad involved massive slave raids against non-Muslim southern peoples (Yoruba, Nupe, many others); Sokoto economy was substantially slave-based. British conquest (1903) is sometimes framed as anti-slavery, but colonial labor policies (forced labor, poll tax) recreated coercion under colonial authority.

sources.citation: Muhammad Bello, Infaq al-Maysur (c. 1813).
Last, M. (1967). The Sokoto Caliphate. Longmans.

main_actor: Idriss Déby Itno (killed); FACT rebels (Front for Change and Concord in Chad)
description: Long-time Chadian president Idriss Déby, who had ruled since 1990, was killed while visiting frontline troops fighting FACT rebels in the Kanem region on 19-20 April 2021 — announced by the army on 20 April, one day after winning a sixth term. His son Mahamat Idriss Déby took power in a military transition. Marked the end of a 31-year authoritarian regime and triggered political instability in the Sahel.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Déby had been a key Western ally (especially for France) against Sahelian jihadist groups, which extended international tolerance of his autocratic style and human rights abuses. The dynastic succession by his son (constitutionally irregular) was accepted by France, AU, and regional actors — illustrating security-first calculus in Sahel policy.

sources.citation: International Crisis Group (2021). Chad After Déby. Africa Briefing N°173.

name_original: South African Truth and Reconciliation Commission begins
location_name: East London, Republic of South Africa
main_actor: Archbishop Desmond Tutu (chair); Alex Boraine (deputy chair); TRC commissioners
description: The South African Truth and Reconciliation Commission began public hearings in East London on 15 April 1996, tasked with investigating human rights abuses during the apartheid era (1960-1994). Over 21,000 victims testified; ~7,000 perpetrators applied for amnesty (1,500 granted). Final report delivered 29 October 1998. Pioneering international model for restorative justice combining truth-telling with conditional amnesty — influenced subsequent commissions in Peru, Sierra Leone, Liberia, Kenya.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the TRC is globally celebrated but criticized within South Africa for inadequate reparations (final payments to victims averaged ~$3,900), failure to prosecute perpetrators who did not apply for amnesty, and insufficient attention to structural economic legacies of apartheid (land ownership, wealth). Some families (Biko, Mxenge) opposed amnesty for their relatives' killers.

sources.citation: Tutu, D. (1999). No Future Without Forgiveness. Doubleday.

main_actor: Agostinho Neto (MPLA, first President); Jonas Savimbi (UNITA, rival claim); Holden Roberto (FNLA, rival claim); Portuguese High Commissioner Leonel Cardoso
description: Angola became formally independent from Portugal on 11 November 1975 — 11 years after the start of the anti-colonial Guerra Colonial (1961). The MPLA proclaimed the People's Republic of Angola in Luanda. UNITA and FNLA proclaimed a rival government in Huambo. Civil war began immediately with Soviet/Cuban support for MPLA and US/South African backing of UNITA. War lasted until 2002 (Savimbi's death), killing ~500,000-800,000. Note: DB has 'independencia de las colonias africanas portuguesas' — likely covers this.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'independencia de las colonias africanas portuguesas'. ETHICS: Angola's independence was a Cold War battlefield — Cuban internationalist soldiers (up to 36,000 at peak) helped defeat South African apartheid army's Operation Savannah, a significant factor in South Africa's eventual democratization. The civil war economy funded by blood diamonds (UNITA) and offshore oil (MPLA) created a rentier state whose inequality (Gini 0.6+) persists.

sources.citation: Gleijeses, P. (2002). Conflicting Missions: Havana, Washington, and Africa, 1959-1976. UNC Press.

name_original: Founding of Cape Colony — Dutch East India Company
calendar_note: Gregorian calendar; proleptic Julian 27 March 1652.
location_name: Cape of Good Hope, southern Africa (Khoikhoi territory)
main_actor: Jan van Riebeeck (Dutch East India Company commander); VOC directors; Khoikhoi pastoralists (Goringhaiqua, Gorinhaicona)
description: Jan van Riebeeck and ~90 VOC employees arrived at Table Bay on 6 April 1652 to establish a refreshment station for ships sailing between Europe and the East Indies. Founded Fort de Goede Hoop (later Cape Town). Within a decade, conflicts with Khoikhoi pastoralists over land and livestock led to the first Khoikhoi-Dutch Wars. Foundation of European settlement in southern Africa that culminated in apartheid. Note: DB has 'kolonie aan die kaap' — possibly YEAR precision; this adds the specific day.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'kolonie aan die kaap'. ETHICS: the 1652 arrival is foundational to Afrikaner nationalism (the 1952 tercentenary celebrated apartheid's opening decade). 6 April was the main public holiday (Van Riebeeck's Day) in apartheid South Africa until 1990. Khoikhoi genocide (smallpox 1713 killed ~90% of local population; territorial expulsion; enslavement of San) is a foundational but often-denied dispossession.

sources.citation: Riebeeck, J. van. Daghregister (1652-62).
Elphick, R. (1977). Kraal and Castle: Khoikhoi and the Founding of White South Africa. Yale UP.

name_original: Samoan independence from New Zealand Trusteeship
main_actor: Malietoa Tanumafili II and Tupua Tamasese Meaole (joint O le Ao o le Malo heads of state); Fiamē Mata'afa Faumuina Mulinu'u II (first Prime Minister); New Zealand (administrator)
description: Western Samoa became independent from New Zealand on 1 January 1962 — the first Pacific Islands state to gain independence in the 20th century. Inherited a distinctive governance structure blending Westminster institutions with traditional matai (chiefly) authority. Dropped 'Western' from name in 1997 (to distinguish from American Samoa). Samoa's path inspired other Pacific decolonization: Nauru (1968), Fiji (1970), Tonga (1970).
ethical_notes: ETHICS: New Zealand's administration of Western Samoa from 1914 (WWI League mandate) included serious abuses — the 1918 influenza mismanagement killed ~22% of Samoans; the 1929 Black Saturday killing of Mau independence leaders. New Zealand formally apologized in 2002. Samoa's communal land tenure (80%+ under matai custody) distinguishes it from neo-colonial land-alienation patterns elsewhere.

sources.citation: Meleisea, M. (1987). The Making of Modern Samoa. University of the South Pacific.

name_original: Ottoman conquest of Mamluk Egypt — Marj Dabiq
calendar_note: Julian calendar. Islamic: 25 Rajab 922 AH.
location_name: Marj Dabiq plain, north of Aleppo, Mamluk Syria
main_actor: Sultan Selim I 'the Grim' of the Ottoman Empire; Mamluk Sultan Qansuh al-Ghawri (killed); Khan al-Bardak (Kurdish ally who defected)
description: Ottoman Sultan Selim I decisively defeated Mamluk Sultan Qansuh al-Ghawri at Marj Dabiq near Aleppo. Ghawri was killed or died of stroke on the field; his army disintegrated due to Kurdish defections and Ottoman firearms advantage. Opened the road to Ottoman conquest of Syria (surrender of Damascus 28 September 1516) and Egypt (Battle of Ridaniya 22 January 1517). Cairo fell 13 April 1517; last Mamluk sultan Tuman bay II executed 14 April 1517.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Marj Dabiq is remembered as the battle that ended ~267 years of Mamluk rule (1250-1517) over Egypt and Syria. Mamluk chroniclers (Ibn Iyas, Ibn Tulun) wrote movingly of the defeat as civilizational catastrophe. Ottoman sources framed it as divine validation of the sultan's universal claim.

sources.citation: Finkel, C. (2005). Osman's Dream: The History of the Ottoman Empire. John Murray.

calendar_note: Julian. Islamic: 2 Rajab 920 AH.
main_actor: Sultan Selim I of the Ottoman Empire; Shah Ismail I of Safavid Persia
description: Ottoman forces under Selim I decisively defeated Safavid forces under Shah Ismail I at Chaldiran on 23 August 1514. Ottoman firearms (matchlock, artillery) devastated the Safavid cavalry, which lacked guns on doctrinal grounds. Shah Ismail was wounded and fled; his favorite wife was captured. Ended the myth of Ismail's invincibility (his Qizilbash followers had considered him semi-divine). Set the Ottoman-Safavid frontier (eastern Anatolia / western Iran) that largely persists today as the Turkey-Iran border. Established the confessional Sunni-Shia state boundary that shapes Middle Eastern geopolitics still.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Selim I's anti-Shia campaign prior to Chaldiran reportedly included the massacre of ~40,000 Anatolian Alevis — a contested figure but foundational to Alevi memory of persecution. The Sunni-Shia confessional geography established in 1514 continues to shape politics from Syria to Iraq to Yemen.

sources.citation: Streusand, D. E. (2011). Islamic Gunpowder Empires: Ottomans, Safavids, and Mughals. Westview.

name_original: First independence declaration of the Netherlands — Act of Abjuration
calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 5 August 1581.
location_name: The Hague, Dutch Republic (then States General of the Seven United Netherlands)
main_actor: States General of the Northern Netherlands; Philip II of Spain (deposed); William the Silent, Prince of Orange
description: The Plakkaat van Verlatinghe (Act of Abjuration), signed 26 July 1581 by the States General of the Seven United Provinces, formally deposed Philip II of Spain as sovereign of the Netherlands on grounds of tyranny. Often cited as one of the formal antecedents of the 1776 American Declaration of Independence (which explicitly cites the 'right to abolish' government by consent). Established the Dutch Republic as independent polity, though Spain formally recognized only in 1648 (Peace of Münster).
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Dutch Revolt was both a war of independence and a religious war (Calvinist north vs Catholic south). Dutch independence was followed by vigorous empire-building (VOC 1602, WIC 1621), with attendant violence in Indonesia (Banda genocide 1621), Taiwan (1624-62), Africa (transatlantic slave trade), and Brazil.

sources.citation: Plakkaat van Verlatinghe, 26 July 1581.
Parker, G. (1977). The Dutch Revolt. Penguin.

name_original: Annular solar eclipse — Thales of Miletus prediction (traditional)
calendar_note: Proleptic Julian. Astronomical back-calculation from Herodotus 1.74 places the eclipse that ended the Battle of Halys (Medes vs Lydians) at 28 May 585 BCE. One of the earliest historical events dated to a single day via astronomy.
main_actor: King Alyattes of Lydia; King Cyaxares of Media; (traditional: Thales of Miletus, predictor)
description: A solar eclipse visible across Anatolia on 28 May 585 BCE caused fighting between Lydians and Medes at the Battle of Halys to cease. Herodotus reports Thales of Miletus had predicted the eclipse — if true, one of the earliest known scientific predictions (probably based on the Saros cycle). The ensuing Treaty of Halys (cemented by dynastic marriage) ended the five-year war. The eclipse is foundational in setting an absolute date for early Greek and Near Eastern chronology.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Thales's 'prediction' is historiographically contested — modern scholars doubt a Milesian philosopher had the mathematical astronomy to predict solar eclipses reliably. The eclipse itself is astronomically secure; its use for political history requires caution. Herodotus wrote ~150 years after the event.

sources.citation: Herodotus, Histories 1.74 (c. 430 BCE).
Stephenson, F. R. (1997). Historical Eclipses and Earth's Rotation. Cambridge UP.

location_name: Tangshan, Hebei Province, People's Republic of China
main_actor: Tangshan Fault (Mw 7.6); Hua Guofeng and Chinese central government; Tangshan city population (~1 million)
description: M7.6 earthquake struck Tangshan at 03:42 local on 28 July 1976. Official death toll 242,769 (mostly in collapsed unreinforced brick housing); some estimates exceed 650,000. Second-deadliest earthquake in recorded history after 1556 Shaanxi. China initially refused international aid under Mao Zedong's succession crisis (Mao died 9 September 1976). The earthquake was read by many Chinese as heavenly disfavor presaging dynastic transition; within months, Mao was dead, the Gang of Four arrested, and the Cultural Revolution effectively over.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: China's initial refusal of international aid — consistent with 'self-reliance' doctrine — may have cost tens of thousands of lives. Casualty data was classified until 1979. Tangshan rebuilding is now cited as Chinese resilience achievement but the slow and politicized response initially was catastrophic.

sources.citation: Palmer, J. (2012). Heaven Cracks, Earth Shakes: The Tangshan Earthquake and the Death of Mao's China. Basic Books.

name_original: Battle of Dien Bien Phu ends
location_name: Dien Bien Phu, Tonkin, French Indochina
main_actor: Võ Nguyên Giáp (Việt Minh commander); General Christian de Castries (French, surrendered)
description: Việt Minh forces under Võ Nguyên Giáp captured the French fortified position at Dien Bien Phu on 7 May 1954 after a 57-day siege. French losses ~2,200 killed, ~11,720 captured; Việt Minh losses ~8,000 killed. The defeat ended French military resistance in Indochina and led directly to the Geneva Accords (21 July 1954) partitioning Vietnam. Catalyst for global decolonization — proof that industrialized European armies could be defeated by well-organized guerrilla/regular hybrid forces. SKIP candidate — DB has 'chiến dịch điện biên phủ' likely YEAR precision.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'chiến dịch điện biên phủ'. ETHICS: the victory's scale (a 'modern Cannae') shocked the West. ~900 Algerian and ~1,500 Senegalese soldiers in the French garrison — colonial auxiliaries — were among the captured, a dynamic of non-European troops fighting for European colonial empires often elided. US refusal to intervene (Operation Vulture) foreshadowed later American Vietnam involvement.

sources.citation: Windrow, M. (2004). The Last Valley: Dien Bien Phu and the French Defeat in Vietnam. Cassell.

calendar_note: Approximate. Archaeological evidence and Spanish records suggest abandonment ~1572, coinciding with Spanish execution of Túpac Amaru I (24 September 1572 at Cusco).
main_actor: Inca ruling lineage (neo-Inca state of Vilcabamba); Spanish viceroy Francisco de Toledo
description: The Inca royal estate of Machu Picchu, built ~1450 during Pachacuti's reign, was abandoned in the late 16th century, approximately when the Spanish crushed the last Inca redoubt at Vilcabamba (1572) and executed the neo-Inca king Túpac Amaru I (24 September 1572). Never discovered by Spanish conquistadors, the site remained preserved — 'rediscovered' by Hiram Bingham in 1911.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: 'rediscovery' by Bingham in 1911 is a colonial framing — local Quechua farmers had known of the site continuously. Yale University's 1912-1916 removals of ~46,000 artifacts were formally returned to Peru only in 2011-12 after ~100 years. Modern Peruvian nation-building co-opted Inca heritage while marginalizing contemporary Quechua communities.

sources.citation: Burger, R. L. & Salazar, L. C. eds. (2004). Machu Picchu: Unveiling the Mystery of the Incas. Yale UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 4 October 1572.
location_name: Plaza de Armas, Cusco, Viceroyalty of Peru
main_actor: Túpac Amaru I, last Sapa Inca of Vilcabamba (beheaded); Viceroy Francisco de Toledo; Felipe II of Spain
description: Túpac Amaru I, last Sapa Inca of the neo-Inca state of Vilcabamba, was beheaded in the main plaza of Cusco on 24 September 1572 after Spanish captured his retreat at Vilcabamba earlier that month. ~15,000-20,000 Indigenous people reportedly attended the execution in silent grief. His speech on the scaffold (in Quechua) affirmed Christian faith but condemned the injustice of his killing. Ended the Inca dynasty's political existence; his name was taken by later resistance leaders (Túpac Amaru II, 1781).
ethical_notes: ETHICS: King Felipe II, upon learning of the execution, reprimanded Viceroy Toledo and forbade him to return to court. Toledo's 'perpetual encomienda' reforms entrenched the coercive labor system (mita) that devastated Andean populations — the Potosí silver mine mita alone killed an estimated 8 million over 260 years.

sources.citation: Guaman Poma de Ayala, Nueva corónica (c. 1615).
Hemming, J. (1970). The Conquest of the Incas. Macmillan.

calendar_note: Gregorian calendar. French Revolutionary calendar: 10 Thermidor An II (arrest 9 Thermidor, execution 10 Thermidor).
location_name: Place de la Révolution, Paris, French Republic
main_actor: Maximilien Robespierre (executed); Louis Antoine de Saint-Just (executed); Jean-Lambert Tallien and Thermidorian Convention members
description: Maximilien Robespierre was guillotined at the Place de la Révolution on 28 July 1794 (10 Thermidor An II) along with ~21 associates including Saint-Just, Couthon, and his brother Augustin. The Thermidorian Reaction arrest had occurred the previous day after a chaotic Convention session (9 Thermidor). Ended the Reign of Terror (September 1793 – July 1794, ~17,000 guillotined officially, ~30,000-50,000 killed in civil war and prison). Marked political swing from Jacobin radicalism to bourgeois Thermidorian reaction.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Terror's death toll was concentrated in the provinces (especially the Vendée, where estimates run to 170,000 killed) rather than Paris. Robespierre's fall is celebrated by liberal historiography; socialist/communist historiography regards him as proto-democrat betrayed by bourgeois reaction. The Thermidorian period's corruption and reactionary violence (White Terror) is less commemorated.

sources.citation: Soboul, A. (1958). Les sans-culottes parisiens en l'an II. Clavreuil.

name_original: Battle of Pavia — Francis I captured
calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 6 March 1525.
main_actor: King Francis I of France (captured); Emperor Charles V (not present); Fernando d'Avalos (commander); Georg von Frundsberg (Landsknechte)
description: Imperial forces (Spanish tercios and German Landsknechte) decisively defeated French army at Pavia on 24 February 1525 — Charles V's 25th birthday. King Francis I of France was captured. ~10,000-14,000 French killed; French nobility decimated. Francis imprisoned in Madrid until humiliating Treaty of Madrid (14 January 1526, later repudiated). First major battle decided by firearms. Cemented Habsburg dominance in Italy for 150 years.
ethical_notes: Francis's famous (possibly apocryphal) letter to his mother — 'Madame, tout est perdu fors l'honneur' ('All is lost save honor') — became a French national meme. The 1525 battle is foundational for narratives of Italian Wars (1494-1559) and early modern firearm transformation of warfare.

sources.citation: Giono, J. (1965). La bataille de Pavie. Gallimard.

calendar_note: Gregorian calendar. Galileo's Sidereus Nuncius observation logs begin 7 January 1610.
main_actor: Galileo Galilei
description: On the evening of 7 January 1610, Galileo Galilei using his 20× telescope observed three small bodies near Jupiter — initially thought to be fixed stars. On 8, 10, 11 January he identified a fourth and noted their orbital motion. Published observations in Sidereus Nuncius (Venice, March 1610). The Medicean moons (Io, Europa, Ganymede, Callisto) were the first satellites of another planet discovered and provided decisive empirical support for Copernican heliocentrism.
ethical_notes: Galileo named the moons 'Medicean stars' to flatter his patron Cosimo II de' Medici, securing a post at the Florentine court. Simon Marius claimed priority for the discovery (28 December 1609 Julian = 7 January 1610 Gregorian); modern historians credit Galileo for first publication but acknowledge Marius's independent observation.

sources.citation: Galilei, G. (1610). Sidereus Nuncius. Venice.

calendar_note: Julian calendar (Old Style); proleptic Gregorian 15 July 1687.
main_actor: Isaac Newton; Edmond Halley (editor and financier)
description: Isaac Newton's Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica was published in London on 5 July 1687. Financed by Edmond Halley out of his own pocket after the Royal Society ran out of funds. Established the three laws of motion, the law of universal gravitation, and the mathematical framework for classical mechanics that dominated physics until Einstein (1905). Arguably the single most influential scientific work ever published.
ethical_notes: Newton's intellectual debts to Galileo, Kepler, Descartes, Hooke, and others are extensive. His priority dispute with Leibniz over calculus soured Anglo-continental relations for decades. Newton was deeply religious (wrote more on alchemy and theology than physics), Warden of the Royal Mint (involved in brutal pursuit of counterfeiters), and held conventional 17th-century views supporting the slave trade (he invested in the South Sea Company).

sources.citation: Newton, I. (1687). Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica.
Westfall, R. S. (1980). Never at Rest: A Biography of Isaac Newton. Cambridge UP.

main_actor: Charles Darwin; John Murray (publisher); Alfred Russel Wallace (independent co-discoverer, precipitated publication)
description: 'On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection' was published by John Murray on 24 November 1859. First print run of 1,250 copies sold out in a day. Darwin had delayed publication for ~20 years but was pushed by receiving Alfred Russel Wallace's 1858 paper outlining essentially the same theory. Transformed biology, philosophy, religion, and social thought. Six editions during Darwin's lifetime.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Darwin's core theory was scientifically revolutionary and is today considered the foundation of modern biology. However, the subsequent appropriation of 'Darwinian' or 'social Darwinist' language (Spencer, not Darwin) to justify colonial conquest, eugenics, and racial hierarchy is a distorted legacy. Darwin himself held 19th-century racial views but was an abolitionist and his theory of common descent undermines biological racism.

sources.citation: Darwin, C. (1859). On the Origin of Species. John Murray.
Browne, J. (2002). Charles Darwin: The Power of Place. Knopf.

location_name: Annalen der Physik editorial office, Leipzig, Germany (author in Bern, Switzerland)
main_actor: Albert Einstein (then a Swiss Patent Office clerk)
description: Albert Einstein's 'Zur Elektrodynamik bewegter Körper' ('On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies'), introducing special relativity, was published in Annalen der Physik vol. 17 on 26 September 1905. Part of his 'Annus Mirabilis' — four papers in 1905 covering the photoelectric effect (for which he later won the 1921 Nobel), Brownian motion, special relativity, and E=mc². Revolutionized physics; foundation for 20th-century developments including nuclear energy and GPS.
ethical_notes: Einstein was a patent clerk, not a university professor, at the time — a reminder that institutional gatekeeping does not always track scientific insight. His later role in advocating nuclear weapons research (1939 Szilárd-Einstein letter to Roosevelt) was based on fear of Nazi nuclear program; Einstein later deeply regretted this.

sources.citation: Einstein, A. (1905). Zur Elektrodynamik bewegter Körper. Annalen der Physik 17.

main_actor: Catherine the Great (Russia); Frederick II (Prussia); Maria Theresa (Austria); Stanisław August Poniatowski (Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth king, unwilling signatory)
description: Treaty signed at Saint Petersburg on 5 August 1772 partitioning ~30% of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth's territory among Russia (92,000 km²), Prussia (36,000 km²), and Austria (83,000 km²). Forced by the three powers' military presence; Polish Sejm ratified under duress 30 September 1773. Second Partition (23 January 1793) and Third Partition (24 October 1795) eliminated Polish statehood entirely until 1918.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Partitions were justified by Enlightenment-era critiques of Polish 'anarchy' (liberum veto, noble constitution) — a classic case of using reformist rhetoric to legitimize imperial aggression. The erasure of Poland from the map for 123 years (1795-1918) shaped modern Polish national identity (messianism, diasporic nationalism) and, via 1939-45 recapitulation, profound wariness of great-power politics.

sources.citation: Lukowski, J. (1999). The Partitions of Poland 1772, 1793, 1795. Longman.

calendar_note: Julian calendar (used by Greek Orthodox Church); Gregorian 6 April 1821. 25 March is now Greek Independence Day. The uprising actually began earlier (22 February 1821 in Moldavia with Ypsilantis).
main_actor: Archbishop Germanos of Old Patras (reputedly blessing the flag); Theodoros Kolokotronis; Bouboulina Laskarina; Alexandros Ypsilantis (earlier revolt in Moldavia)
description: Traditional date of Greek declaration of independence from the Ottoman Empire, 25 March 1821 (Annunciation). Modern historians note the revolt actually began earlier with Alexandros Ypsilantis crossing the Pruth (22 February 1821). Greek War of Independence (1821-1829) killed ~25,000 Greeks and ~15,000 Turks (Chios massacre 1822 by Ottomans; Tripolitsa massacre 1821 by Greeks). International recognition at Protocol of London (1830), Greek Kingdom founded 1832.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the war was foundational to modern Greek national identity and influential across Balkan nationalisms. Romantic philhellenism (Byron, Delacroix's Massacre at Chios) mobilized European sympathy. The Tripolitsa massacre (September 1821) killed ~8,000-15,000 Turks and Jews and is less remembered in Greek nationalist historiography. Ottoman reprisals (Chios 1822, ~25,000 killed) are well-memorialized.

sources.citation: Clogg, R. (2013). A Concise History of Greece. Cambridge UP.

main_actor: Parisian National Guard; Adolphe Thiers (government at Versailles); Montmartre crowd; General Claude Lecomte and General Clément-Thomas (killed 18 March)
description: The Paris Commune began 18 March 1871 when Montmartre workers and National Guard refused to surrender cannons to regular army under orders from Thiers. Generals Lecomte and Clément-Thomas were lynched. The Commune governed Paris until 28 May 1871. 'Bloody Week' (Semaine sanglante, 21-28 May) saw ~15,000-20,000 Communards killed by regular army; ~43,000 arrested. Karl Marx wrote 'The Civil War in France' (1871) celebrating the Commune as the first workers' government. Influenced subsequent revolutionary movements globally.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Communards' reforms (8-hour day, women's suffrage proposal, church-state separation) were radical for 1871. The scale of the government's repression exceeded even Revolutionary Terror — an estimated 25,000 dead in a week, mostly worker civilians. French Republican memory long downplayed the massacre; recent reckonings (François Furet's critique; Quentin Deluermoz's scholarship) have restored it.

sources.citation: Tombs, R. (1999). The Paris Commune 1871. Longman.

location_name: Imperial Palace, Tokyo, Empire of Japan
main_actor: Emperor Meiji; Itō Hirobumi (principal drafter); Inoue Kowashi, Hermann Roesler (constitutional advisers)
description: The Constitution of the Empire of Japan (大日本帝国憲法) was promulgated by Emperor Meiji on 11 February 1889 (traditionally considered the anniversary of Jimmu's enthronement in 660 BCE). Drafted primarily by Itō Hirobumi, modeled on the Prussian constitution of 1850. Established a constitutional monarchy with the emperor as 'sacred and inviolable' head of state, a bicameral Diet, and individual rights subject to statutory limits. Lasted until the 1947 postwar constitution.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Meiji Constitution modernized Japanese governance but retained extensive imperial prerogatives that would be used by military cabinets in the 1930s to override civilian control. The emperor's 'sacred' status enabled the ideological apparatus of wartime ultra-nationalism. The 1947 US-guided constitution reversed these elements, introducing popular sovereignty and renouncing war (Article 9).

sources.citation: Beckmann, G. M. (1957). The Making of the Meiji Constitution. Kansas UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar. Lactantius and Eusebius date to 28 October 312.
location_name: Milvian Bridge over the Tiber, Rome
main_actor: Constantine I; Maxentius (killed, drowned in Tiber)
description: Constantine I defeated Maxentius at the Milvian Bridge, consolidating control of the Western Roman Empire. Per Lactantius and Eusebius, Constantine reportedly had a vision before the battle — a cross (or Chi-Rho) and the words 'in hoc signo vinces' ('by this sign thou shalt conquer') — leading to his decision to mark his army's shields. The battle is conventionally seen as the turning point toward Christianization of the empire.
ethical_notes: Constantine's 'conversion' was gradual — he continued solar imagery (Sol Invictus) for years after 312. The vision narrative is retrospective (Eusebius's Vita Constantini c. 337). Full Christianization of the Empire came with Theodosius's 380 Edict of Thessalonica ('edictum thessalonicense cunctos populos' — already in DB).

sources.citation: Lactantius, De mortibus persecutorum 44 (c. 314).
Potter, D. S. (2013). Constantine the Emperor. Oxford UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar.
location_name: Basilica of St. Euphemia, Chalcedon (Kadıköy), Eastern Roman Empire
main_actor: Emperor Marcian and Empress Pulcheria; Patriarchs Anatolius of Constantinople, Dioscorus of Alexandria (deposed); ~520 bishops
description: Fourth Ecumenical Council, opening 8 October 451 in Chalcedon. The Chalcedonian Definition proclaimed Christ as one person in two natures, 'without confusion, change, division, or separation'. Rejected Monophysitism (Eutychian teaching). The theological split triggered the Oriental Orthodox schism — Coptic, Syriac Orthodox, Armenian, Ethiopian, and Eritrean churches remain non-Chalcedonian to this day (~85 million Christians).
ethical_notes: The Chalcedonian schism was partly theological, partly political — it aligned with imperial power politics between Rome-Constantinople and Alexandria-Antioch. Imperial persecution of non-Chalcedonians in Egypt and Syria arguably eased the 7th-century Arab Islamic conquest by alienating these provinces.

sources.citation: Acts of the Council of Chalcedon, trans. Price & Gaddis (2005), Liverpool UP.
Meyendorff, J. (1989). Imperial Unity and Christian Divisions. St Vladimir's.

calendar_note: Julian calendar. Cardinal Humbert placed the bull of excommunication on the altar of Hagia Sophia on Saturday 16 July 1054. Patriarch Michael Cerularius reciprocated later that month.
main_actor: Cardinal Humbert of Silva Candida (papal legate); Patriarch Michael Cerularius; Pope Leo IX (already dead, 19 April 1054)
description: Cardinal Humbert, papal legate, placed a bull of excommunication against Patriarch Michael Cerularius on the altar of Hagia Sophia during the Divine Liturgy on 16 July 1054. Cerularius reciprocated on 24 July 1054. Conventional marker of the Great Schism separating Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox Christianity (~1.3 billion and ~260 million adherents today). Mutual excommunications were rescinded only in 1965 (Paul VI and Athenagoras I).
