ethical_notes: The 'schism' was a process, not a single event — theological (filioque clause, azymes), ritual, and linguistic divergences had accumulated for centuries. The 1204 Fourth Crusade sack of Constantinople cemented the separation far more than 1054. Russian, Greek, Arabic, and African Orthodoxy preserve the Byzantine tradition as parallel global Christianity.

sources.citation: Siecienski, A. E. (2019). The Papacy and the Orthodox. Oxford UP.

calendar_note: Julian calendar. Al-Tabari dates the battle to Safar 37 AH (July 657 CE).
main_actor: Caliph Ali ibn Abi Talib; Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan (governor of Syria)
description: Caliph Ali ibn Abi Talib's forces engaged Mu'awiya's Syrian army at Siffin on the upper Euphrates (July 657). After ~3 months of skirmishing and inconclusive fighting, Mu'awiya's troops raised Qur'an pages on their spears demanding arbitration, which Ali reluctantly accepted. The arbitration decision and the subsequent emergence of the Kharijites led directly to Ali's assassination (661) and Mu'awiya's establishment of the Umayyad dynasty. Foundational to the Sunni-Shia divide.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Sunni and Shia traditions remember Siffin very differently. Shia view Ali's cause as just and the arbitration as betrayal; Sunni tradition preserves respect for both parties as Companions. Al-Tabari's 10th-century account is the main connected narrative, built from earlier oral traditions.

sources.citation: al-Tabari, Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk, vol. 17 trans. Hawting (1996), SUNY.
Madelung, W. (1997). The Succession to Muhammad. Cambridge UP.

name_original: Muhammad's farewell pilgrimage — Ghadir Khumm sermon
calendar_note: Julian. Islamic: 18 Dhu'l-Hijja 10 AH (=10 March 632 CE Julian per standard conversion).
location_name: Ghadir Khumm, Hejaz (between Mecca and Medina)
main_actor: Prophet Muhammad; ~120,000 pilgrims (per Shia tradition); Ali ibn Abi Talib
description: On 18 Dhu'l-Hijja 10 AH, during his farewell pilgrimage, Muhammad is reported in Shia tradition to have declared at Ghadir Khumm: 'He whose master (mawla) I am, Ali is his master'. Shia Islam treats this as the explicit designation of Ali as Muhammad's successor. Sunni tradition accepts the event but interprets the statement as a statement of affection, not political succession. Foundational to Shia theology; commemorated as Eid al-Ghadir.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: This event is among the most contested in Islamic history. Shia historiography gives it central status; Sunni historiography accepts the hadith but contests the Shia interpretation. Modern AtlasPI must preserve both framings without arbitration.

sources.citation: al-Tabari, Tarikh, vol. 9.
Dakake, M. M. (2007). The Charismatic Community: Shi'ite Identity in Early Islam. SUNY Press.

name_original: Massacre of Beth Yerah / Kerkh — Crusade capture of Jerusalem
calendar_note: Julian calendar. Crusader sources (Gesta Francorum, Raymond of Aguilers) and Arab sources (Ibn al-Athir, Ibn al-Qalanisi) converge on the date.
main_actor: First Crusaders under Godfrey of Bouillon, Raymond of Saint-Gilles, Tancred; Fatimid garrison under Iftikhar al-Dawla
description: First Crusader army captured Jerusalem after a 5-week siege. Three days of massacre followed: Crusader sources describe streets 'ankle-deep' or 'bridle-deep' in blood; Arab sources (Ibn al-Athir) estimate 70,000 Muslim dead. Jewish residents who took refuge in the main synagogue were burned alive. Estimates vary widely: modern scholars propose 3,000-30,000 killed. Foundation of the Kingdom of Jerusalem (1099-1187, 1192-1291).
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the 1099 massacre shaped Islamic memory of Crusader violence for centuries; Saladin's relative clemency at the 1187 reconquest was explicitly contrasted with 1099. Modern uses of 'Crusader' as anti-Muslim dogwhistle draw on 1099 imagery. The Jewish community of Jerusalem was entirely destroyed, reconstituted only after 1187.

sources.citation: Raymond of Aguilers, Historia Francorum qui ceperunt Iherusalem (c. 1100).
Ibn al-Athir, al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh.
Kedar, B. Z. (2004). The Jerusalem Massacre of July 1099. Crusades 3.

calendar_note: Julian calendar. Islamic: 17 Ramadan 926 AH. Sulayman acceded 30 September 1520 upon Selim I's death.
main_actor: Sultan Sulayman I 'the Magnificent' / Kanuni; preceded Selim I (died 22 September 1520)
description: Sulayman I, 'the Magnificent' (Kanuni, 'the Lawgiver' in Turkish), acceded to the Ottoman throne on 30 September 1520. His 46-year reign (until 1566) saw maximum Ottoman expansion: Belgrade (1521), Rhodes (1522), Mohács (1526), first Vienna siege (1529), and legal codification. Note: DB has 'الخلافة العثمانية — سليمان القانوني' but likely YEAR; adding specific accession date.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'الخلافة العثمانية — سليمان القانوني'. ETHICS: Ottoman imperial historiography long minimized the devşirme (child levy) system that staffed elite janissary and bureaucratic corps from enslaved Christian Balkan and Anatolian boys. Contemporary Ottoman chronicles (Peçevi, Selaniki) celebrate Sulayman as apogee; European observers also praised him.

sources.citation: Imber, C. (2019). The Ottoman Empire, 1300-1650: The Structure of Power. Palgrave.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 16 May 1527.
main_actor: Charles de Bourbon (killed at start of assault); Charles V Habsburg's Imperial army (~20,000 unpaid mutinous troops incl. Landsknechte); Pope Clement VII (fled to Castel Sant'Angelo)
description: Unpaid mutinous Imperial troops (Spanish tercios and German Landsknechte, many Lutheran) stormed Rome on 6 May 1527. Eight days of sacking, massacre, and desecration followed. ~12,000-45,000 killed; churches looted; nuns raped; relics destroyed. Pope Clement VII sheltered in Castel Sant'Angelo. Effectively ended the High Renaissance in Rome and weakened papal political independence. Cemented Habsburg dominance in Italy.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the sack was commanded by the Holy Roman Emperor against the Catholic Pope — illustrating that 'Christian' political violence was not limited to interconfessional conflict. Lutheran Landsknechte took special pleasure in desecrating Catholic sites (Luther's name graffitied on Raphael's Vatican frescoes). Italian memory regards 1527 as civilizational catastrophe comparable to 410.

sources.citation: Hook, J. (1972). The Sack of Rome, 1527. Macmillan.

name_original: Fall of Jerusalem to Romans — destruction of Second Temple
calendar_note: Julian calendar. Hebrew: 9-10 Av 3830 (Jewish tradition commemorates Tisha b'Av). Josephus gives 10 Loos (Macedonian) = 29 August. SKIP candidate — DB has 'חורבן בית ראשון' (First Temple destruction, different event); this Second Temple destruction may be missing.
main_actor: Titus (later emperor), son of Vespasian; Zealot and Sicarii defenders; civilian Jewish population
description: Roman legions under Titus breached the Temple Mount on 29 August 70 CE (9 Av). The Second Temple was destroyed by fire. Josephus (Bellum Judaicum) records ~1,100,000 killed in the siege and ~97,000 enslaved; modern estimates are lower but still catastrophic. End of the First Jewish-Roman War (66-73). Fundamentally transformed Judaism (shift from Temple cult to rabbinic/diasporic form) and provided Christianity a separation catalyst from Judaism. 9 Av (Tisha b'Av) remains the Jewish day of mourning.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Josephus wrote as Roman client — his accounts are not neutral. The Arch of Titus in Rome commemorates the triumph with an image of the menorah being carried away; Jewish communities traditionally did not walk under the arch until Israel's founding (1948) reversed the symbolism. The destruction of the Temple is foundational to Jewish liturgical year; rebuilding it remains a millenarian expectation in some religious currents, with modern political implications for Al-Aqsa.

sources.citation: Josephus, Bellum Judaicum VI (c. 75 CE).
Goodman, M. (2007). Rome and Jerusalem: The Clash of Ancient Civilizations. Allen Lane.

name_original: Birth of the Republic of Turkey
main_actor: Mustafa Kemal (Atatürk); İsmet İnönü; Grand National Assembly
description: The Turkish Grand National Assembly proclaimed the Republic of Turkey on 29 October 1923, ending the Ottoman Empire's ~623-year existence (the sultanate had been abolished 1 November 1922; the caliphate followed 3 March 1924). Mustafa Kemal elected first president. Initiated the Kemalist reforms: Latin alphabet (1928), secularism (1937), women's suffrage (1934). Note: DB has 'lozan antlaşması' (Lausanne treaty) but likely not the Republic proclamation.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Republic's founding followed the Armenian Genocide (already in DB as 'հայոց ցեղասպանություն'), the Greek-Turkish population exchange (~1.6 million displaced), and the Pontic Greek Genocide. Kurdish uprisings (Sheikh Said 1925, Dersim 1937-38) were crushed with mass violence still contested in modern Turkish politics.

sources.citation: Zürcher, E. J. (2017). Turkey: A Modern History, 4th ed. I. B. Tauris.

calendar_note: Julian calendar. St Crispin's Day.
location_name: Agincourt (Azincourt), County of Artois, Kingdom of France
main_actor: King Henry V of England; Constable Charles d'Albret (killed); Duke of Orleans (captured)
description: English army of ~6,000 (mostly longbowmen) defeated French army of ~20,000-30,000 at Agincourt on St Crispin's Day. French chivalry catastrophically charged through muddy fields into English arrow storm. ~6,000 French killed including 3 dukes and 90 barons; ~1,500 captured. English losses ~400. Henry V ordered execution of most French prisoners fearing fresh attack. Major step toward English conquest of northern France. Immortalized in Shakespeare's Henry V.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Henry V's prisoner execution order was unusual even by medieval laws of war — a war crime by any standard. Shakespeare's 'We few, we happy few' framing obscures the extraordinarily asymmetric casualties and the fact that the English campaign's purpose was dynastic: Henry claimed the French crown via his great-great-grandfather Edward II.

sources.citation: Barker, J. (2005). Agincourt: The King, the Campaign, the Battle. Little, Brown.

name_original: Joan of Arc executed at Rouen
calendar_note: Julian calendar.
main_actor: Joan of Arc / Jeanne d'Arc (burned at stake, aged 19); Bishop Pierre Cauchon (presiding); King Henry VI of England
description: Jeanne d'Arc was burned at the stake at the Old Market of Rouen on 30 May 1431 after a politically-motivated heresy trial. She had helped lift the Siege of Orleans (May 1429) and enabled Charles VII's coronation at Reims (17 July 1429). Captured by Burgundians (23 May 1430), sold to English, convicted on 70 charges reduced to 12. A 1456 retrial posthumously annulled the conviction. Canonized 1920.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Joan's trial was procedurally irregular even by 15th-century standards (denial of female jurors, assessors not ecclesiastical judges, recantation extracted under duress then 'relapse' manufactured). French nationalist mythology from Michelet onward valorizes her; modern scholarship emphasizes the political theater of English-Burgundian need to discredit Charles VII's sacral kingship.

sources.citation: Procès de condamnation de Jeanne d'Arc, ed. Tisset & Lanhers (1960-71).
Pernoud, R. & Clin, M.-V. (1998). Joan of Arc: Her Story. St Martin's.

name_original: Black Death arrives in Sicily — Messina
calendar_note: Julian calendar. Michele da Piazza (14th c. Franciscan chronicler) dates the arrival of twelve Genoese galleys to October 1347.
main_actor: Yersinia pestis (bubonic plague bacterium); Genoese galleys from Caffa; Mongol siege host at Caffa (origin)
description: Twelve Genoese trading galleys arrived at Messina in October 1347 with infected sailors — introducing the Black Death to Europe. Within four years, ~30-60% of Europe's population died (~75-200 million deaths worldwide). The plague reshaped European labor markets (Statute of Labourers 1351), religion (flagellant movement, pogroms against Jews accused of poisoning wells), and demographic distribution. Note: DB has 'mors nigra' — possibly YEAR or range precision.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'mors nigra'. ETHICS: the Black Death triggered massive antisemitic violence across Europe (Strasbourg pogrom 14 February 1349 killed ~2,000 Jews, Basel, Cologne, Mainz). Pope Clement VI's 1348 bull protecting Jews was widely ignored. The 'Arabic silence' thesis (no Arab plague chronicles) has been rebutted (Dols 1977); Middle Eastern accounts of the pandemic are substantial.

sources.citation: Michele da Piazza, Cronaca (c. 1361).
Benedictow, O. J. (2004). The Black Death 1346-1353: The Complete History. Boydell.

calendar_note: Julian calendar.
main_actor: King Philip II Augustus of France; Otto IV Holy Roman Emperor (fled); Count Ferrand of Flanders (captured); King John of England (not present, consequences)
description: Philip II Augustus of France decisively defeated a coalition of Emperor Otto IV, Count Ferrand of Flanders, and English-allied troops at Bouvines in Flanders on Sunday 27 July 1214. Ended Plantagenet claims in northern France. The defeat undermined John of England (losing his continental allies) and directly contributed to the Magna Carta crisis the following year. Foundational for French royal consolidation.
ethical_notes: Georges Duby's classic 'Le Dimanche de Bouvines' (1973) uses the battle as window onto medieval mentalities — the first battle in French history with a clear specific date in lay memory, and the first for which royal propaganda immediately produced a version of events.

sources.citation: Duby, G. (1973). Le Dimanche de Bouvines. Gallimard.

calendar_note: Julian calendar. Serbian Vidovdan (St Vitus's Day). Islamic: 8 Shaban 791 AH.
location_name: Kosovo Polje (Field of Blackbirds), Principality of Serbia
main_actor: Prince Lazar Hrebeljanović of Serbia (killed); Sultan Murad I of the Ottoman Empire (killed by Miloš Obilić); Prince Vlatko Vuković of Bosnia
description: Battle between Ottoman forces under Sultan Murad I and a Christian (primarily Serb and Bosnian) coalition under Prince Lazar on 15 June 1389. Both leaders killed — Murad by the Serbian knight Miloš Obilić; Lazar captured and executed. Ottoman victory, though the battle did not immediately end Serbian statehood (final Ottoman conquest 1459). The date (Vidovdan) became foundational to Serbian national identity, revived in 19th-century Romantic nationalism and politicized by Slobodan Milošević in his 28 June 1989 Gazimestan speech preceding the Yugoslav wars.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the 1389 Battle of Kosovo has been mythologized far beyond its military significance. The 'Kosovo myth' frames Serbs as Christian martyrs defending Europe, crafted in the Kosovo cycle epic poetry (19th c. Vuk Karadžić collections). Milošević's 1989 Gazimestan speech used the 600th anniversary to foreshadow ethnic conflict. Kosovar Albanian memory and historiography emphasize their own presence in the region and reject Serbian primacy narratives.

sources.citation: Emmert, T. A. (1990). Serbian Golgotha: Kosovo, 1389. Columbia UP.

name_original: Ottoman conquest of Egypt — Battle of Ridaniya
calendar_note: Julian calendar. Islamic: 29 Dhu'l-Hijjah 922 AH.
main_actor: Sultan Selim I 'the Grim' of the Ottoman Empire; Sultan Tuman bay II of the Mamluk Sultanate (defeated, executed 14 April 1517)
description: Ottoman sultan Selim I defeated Mamluk sultan Tuman bay II at Ridaniya outside Cairo on 22 January 1517, following his earlier victory at Marj Dabiq (24 August 1516). Tuman bay was captured, tried, hanged at Cairo's Bab Zuwayla on 14 April 1517. Ended ~267 years of Mamluk rule (1250-1517). Made Ottoman sultan custodian of Mecca and Medina (as Khadim al-Haramain al-Sharifain). Incorporated Egypt, Syria, and the Hijaz into the Ottoman Empire — shaping the Sunni Muslim world's political geography for the next four centuries.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Ottoman rule in Egypt from 1517 transitioned the country from directly Mamluk-ruled to provincial governance, with the Mamluks retained as local military elite — a hybrid that collapsed with Napoleon's invasion (1798) and Muhammad Ali's 1811 Citadel Massacre of the Mamluks. Modern Egyptian historiography variably frames 1517 as conquest, integration, or mere dynastic succession.

sources.citation: Petry, C. F. (1993). Protectors or Praetorians? The Last Mamlūk Sultans and Egypt's Waning as a Great Power. SUNY Press.

name_original: Opening of the Transcontinental Railroad — Promontory Summit
location_name: Promontory Summit, Utah Territory, United States
main_actor: Leland Stanford (Central Pacific); Thomas Durant (Union Pacific); thousands of Chinese and Irish laborers
description: The 'Golden Spike' ceremony on 10 May 1869 at Promontory Summit connected the Central Pacific and Union Pacific railroads, completing the first US transcontinental railway. Reduced US coast-to-coast travel from ~6 months (Oregon Trail, or sea voyage via Panama or Cape Horn) to 6 days. Enabled mass settlement of the Great Plains and Pacific coast — and accelerated dispossession of Indigenous nations.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: ~15,000 Chinese laborers (majority of Central Pacific workforce) were omitted from the iconic ceremony photograph. Chinese workers faced dangerous conditions in the Sierra Nevada tunnels (estimated 1,200 deaths). The Railway enabled the annihilation of the buffalo herds (~50 million in 1850 to ~1,000 by 1880), devastating Plains Indigenous economies and war-making capacity.

sources.citation: Bain, D. H. (1999). Empire Express: Building the First Transcontinental Railroad. Viking.

main_actor: Admiral Chester Nimitz, Rear Admirals Raymond Spruance, Frank Fletcher (US); Admiral Yamamoto Isoroku, Vice Admiral Nagumo Chūichi (Japan)
description: US Navy decisively defeated the Imperial Japanese Navy's 1st Mobile Force at Midway on 4-7 June 1942. Four Japanese fleet carriers (Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, Hiryu) sunk; one US carrier (Yorktown) sunk. Losses: ~3,057 Japanese, 307 US personnel. Strategic turning point of the Pacific War — Japan never recovered carrier striking power. US codebreaking (Station HYPO in Hawaii) deciphered Japanese plans, enabling the ambush.
ethical_notes: Midway is sometimes celebrated as US strategic brilliance; historians note that Japanese operational mistakes (divided forces, inadequate search patterns, fatal 5-minute delay in rearming planes) were at least as decisive. The Pacific War killed ~26 million people, overwhelmingly Asian civilians; Midway's contribution to ending that suffering came via years of subsequent island campaigns and, controversially, atomic bombings.

sources.citation: Parshall, J. & Tully, A. (2005). Shattered Sword: The Untold Story of the Battle of Midway. Potomac Books.

name_original: Schuman Declaration — European Coal and Steel Community
location_name: Salon de l'Horloge, Quai d'Orsay, Paris
main_actor: Robert Schuman (French Foreign Minister); Jean Monnet (drafter); Konrad Adenauer (West German Chancellor, concurring)
description: French Foreign Minister Robert Schuman proposed on 9 May 1950 the pooling of French and German coal and steel production under a common High Authority — the foundational step of what became the European Union. The proposal, drafted by Jean Monnet, aimed to make war between France and Germany 'not merely unthinkable but materially impossible'. The European Coal and Steel Community was established by the Treaty of Paris (18 April 1951). 9 May is Europe Day.
ethical_notes: The Schuman Declaration's careful pragmatism (start with coal and steel, avoid grand political federalism) shaped the EU's 'functionalist' integration method for decades. The choice of 9 May 1950 was not accidental — 5 years to the day after VE Day. The declaration's language of peace through economic interdependence has been both celebrated (70 years of intra-EU peace) and criticized (democratic deficit in subsequent institutions).

sources.citation: Dinan, D. (2004). Europe Recast: A History of European Union. Palgrave.

name_original: World Wide Web — public launch on NeXT
main_actor: Tim Berners-Lee; Robert Cailliau; CERN
description: Tim Berners-Lee publicly announced the World Wide Web project on the alt.hypertext newsgroup on 6 August 1991, including a summary, URL of the first website (info.cern.ch), and instructions to download the WorldWideWeb browser software for NeXT computers. SKIP candidate — DB has 'world wide web proposal' and 'creation of the internet / arpanet → world wide web'. Flagging.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'world wide web proposal'. The Web's 1991 launch was quiet (a Usenet post) but reshaped modern life. CERN's April 1993 decision to release WWW technology royalty-free was decisive. Berners-Lee's later work on decentralized web (Solid) reflects his unease with centralized platform dominance.

sources.citation: Berners-Lee, T. & Fischetti, M. (1999). Weaving the Web. HarperOne.

calendar_note: Proleptic Julian. Egyptian Ramesside inscriptions date to the 5th year, 3rd month of shemu of Ramesses II (= approximately May-June 1274 BCE). Alternative chronologies give 1275 or 1285 BCE.
main_actor: Pharaoh Ramesses II of the Nineteenth Dynasty of Egypt; King Muwatalli II of the Hittite Empire
description: Largest chariot battle of antiquity between the Egyptian Empire under Ramesses II and the Hittite Empire under Muwatalli II. ~5,000-6,000 chariots involved. Tactical stalemate — though Egyptian propaganda claimed triumph. Resulted in the Egyptian-Hittite peace treaty (1258 BCE), the earliest surviving international peace treaty, a copy of which hangs at UN headquarters. Note: DB has 'shalmu sa qadesh' — possibly without day/month precision.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — 'shalmu sa qadesh' appears in DB. ETHICS: Egyptian inscriptions (Poem of Pentaur, Bulletin) dominate the historiography with a self-serving narrative; Hittite cuneiform tablets from Boğazkale provide the counter-narrative. Both framings are state propaganda; neither is neutral.

sources.citation: Ramesses II, Kadesh inscriptions (Luxor, Karnak, Abu Simbel).
Spalinger, A. J. (2005). War in Ancient Egypt. Blackwell.

name_original: Kublai Khan enthroned as Great Khan
calendar_note: Julian calendar. Chinese sources (Yuan Shi) date Kublai's Khuriltai to Kaiping (Shangdu) in the summer of Zhongtong 1 / Year of the Metal Monkey — 5 May 1260 corresponds to the 24th day, 3rd month.
main_actor: Kublai Khan; Ariq Böke (rival brother, contested)
description: Kublai Khan was proclaimed Great Khan at a Khuriltai at Kaiping on 5 May 1260, defying his younger brother Ariq Böke (elected Great Khan by a rival Khuriltai at Karakorum). The resulting Toluid Civil War (1260-64) split the Mongol Empire. Kublai founded the Yuan dynasty (1271) and completed the conquest of Southern Song (1279) — unifying China under Mongol rule for the first time.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Mongol conquest of Song China killed an estimated 30-40 million people (Rossabi 1988, revising earlier inflated totals). Kublai's reign introduced drama, paper currency innovations, and Sino-Persian cultural transmission (Rashid al-Din's Jami' al-tawarikh), but also four-tier ethnic hierarchy (Mongol > Semu > northern Han > southern Han) embedding ethnic discrimination.

sources.citation: Yuan Shi 元史 (compiled 1370).
Rossabi, M. (1988). Khubilai Khan: His Life and Times. California UP.

calendar_note: Gregorian calendar. Chinese lunar: 10th day, 12th month of Daoguang 30.
location_name: Jintian, Guiping, Guangxi Province, Qing Empire
main_actor: Hong Xiuquan (self-proclaimed Heavenly King); Yang Xiuqing, Shi Dakai (generals)
description: Hong Xiuquan proclaimed the Heavenly Kingdom of Great Peace (太平天囯) at Jintian, Guangxi, on 11 January 1851. Hong claimed to be the younger brother of Jesus Christ and waged a revolutionary war combining millenarian Christianity, Chinese millenarian tradition, and social revolution (land redistribution, gender equality, abolition of opium, footbinding, and slavery). Civil war against the Qing lasted until 1864 (fall of Nanjing). ~20-30 million dead — one of the deadliest conflicts in human history.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Taiping Rebellion's death toll approaches WWII's; it remains one of the least-known mega-events in Western historiography. British and French interventions (1860-64) assisted Qing suppression. Taiping social reforms (women's rights, equal inheritance, economic egalitarianism) anticipate later revolutionary movements. Mao Zedong cited Hong Xiuquan as precursor.

sources.citation: Spence, J. D. (1996). God's Chinese Son: The Taiping Heavenly Kingdom of Hong Xiuquan. Norton.

location_name: Birla House, New Delhi, Dominion of India
main_actor: Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi (killed); Nathuram Godse (assassin, Hindu Mahasabha)
description: Mohandas Gandhi was shot three times at point-blank range by Nathuram Godse at 17:17 on 30 January 1948 during his evening prayer meeting at Birla House, New Delhi. Godse, a Hindu nationalist associated with the Hindu Mahasabha, objected to Gandhi's accommodation toward Muslims and Pakistan. Gandhi died within an hour. Godse was tried and executed 15 November 1949. Gandhi's death shocked India and the world; his legacy of nonviolent resistance inspired MLK, Mandela, and many others.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Gandhi's assassination by a Hindu nationalist remains politically sensitive in modern India, where elements of the BJP and RSS trace ideological ancestry to organizations (Hindu Mahasabha, RSS) briefly banned after his death. Godse has been quietly rehabilitated in some Hindutva circles. Modi government's 2018 inauguration of a Godse temple in Gwalior generated international controversy.

sources.citation: Guha, R. (2018). Gandhi: The Years That Changed the World 1914-1948. Knopf.

name_original: King Leopold II takes over Congo Free State
location_name: Congo Free State (personally owned by Leopold II of Belgium)
main_actor: Leopold II of Belgium; International African Association; Henry Morton Stanley (agent)
description: Following recognition at the Berlin Conference (February 1885), Leopold II proclaimed himself sovereign of the État indépendant du Congo on 1 July 1885 — a personal colony, not a Belgian state possession. Over 23 years of Leopoldian rule, an estimated 5-10 million Congolese died from forced labor (rubber collection), disease, starvation, and direct violence. Belgian parliament forced Leopold to transfer sovereignty to Belgium in 1908 after international protest. Note: DB has 'état indépendant du congo' — possibly YEAR precision; this adds the proclamation day.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'état indépendant du congo'. ETHICS-002: the Congo Free State is one of the most systematically documented colonial atrocities. Morel's CRA (1904), Roger Casement's consular report (1904), Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1902), and countless Congolese voices (occluded in colonial archives) testify. Belgium formally apologized only in 2020 (King Philippe regret, not formal apology).

sources.citation: Hochschild, A. (1998). King Leopold's Ghost: A Story of Greed, Terror, and Heroism in Colonial Africa. Mariner.

name_original: Mau Mau uprising — State of Emergency declared
main_actor: Governor Sir Evelyn Baring; Jomo Kenyatta (arrested 21 October); Kenya Land and Freedom Army (Mau Mau); Dedan Kimathi
description: British colonial governor Evelyn Baring declared a State of Emergency in Kenya Colony on 20 October 1952, triggered by Mau Mau attacks on colonial infrastructure and settlers. Mass detention operations (Operation Jock Scott) arrested Kenyatta and ~180 KAU/Kikuyu leaders. Emergency lasted until 12 January 1960. ~11,000-90,000 Kikuyu killed by British forces and loyalists; ~1.5 million Kikuyu detained in screening villages; ~32 European settlers killed. British emergency-era documents were destroyed or concealed; survivors won apology and £19.9 million compensation from UK government in 2013.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Mau Mau emergency is now recognized as a war of colonial counterinsurgency with widespread torture (castration, rape, beating) documented in the 'Hanslope disclosures' (2011). British memory long emphasized Mau Mau 'savagery' while concealing state-sponsored violence against Kikuyu civilians. Academic reckoning (Anderson 2005, Elkins 2005) transformed understanding. 1960 Kenya independence under Kenyatta, previously jailed as 'Mau Mau leader', came 8 years after the Emergency began.

sources.citation: Elkins, C. (2005). Britain's Gulag: The Brutal End of Empire in Kenya. Jonathan Cape.
Anderson, D. (2005). Histories of the Hanged. Weidenfeld & Nicolson.

name_original: Captain Cook lands at Botany Bay
location_name: Kamay (Botany Bay), Gweagal country, eastern Australia
main_actor: Lieutenant James Cook, HMS Endeavour; Joseph Banks, Daniel Solander (naturalists); Gweagal people of the Dharawal nation
description: HMS Endeavour under Lt James Cook made landfall at Kamay (later named Botany Bay) on 29 April 1770. Gweagal warriors resisted the landing; Cook's party fired, wounding one. Cook charted the east coast, claimed 'New South Wales' for Britain (22 August 1770, Possession Island). His charts enabled the 1788 First Fleet colonization (already in DB). Note: DB does not appear to have this arrival; adding as new.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Cook's instructions from the Admiralty included taking possession 'with the Consent of the Natives' — a condition Cook ignored. Aboriginal oral tradition and written records (Bungaree, Yemmerrawanne) preserve alternative accounts. The bullet-wounded Gweagal man and the stolen shield (held in the British Museum until 2022 negotiated return) have become symbols of the contested encounter.

sources.citation: Cook, J. The Journals of Captain James Cook (ed. J. C. Beaglehole, Hakluyt Society 1955-67).
Thomas, N. (2018). Discoveries: The Voyages of Captain Cook. Allen Lane.

main_actor: Captain Samuel Wallis, HMS Dolphin; Queen Purea of Papara
description: Samuel Wallis sighted Tahiti on 18 June 1767 and landed 19 June — first European contact with the Society Islands. Wallis was followed by Bougainville (April 1768) and Cook (April 1769, for the transit of Venus observation). European contact introduced metal tools, new foods — and devastating disease, firearms, and alcohol. Population collapse ~80% by 1800.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Wallis's crew fired cannon at Tahitian waka canoes approaching the Dolphin, killing an unknown number. European 'discovery' framing erases ~2,000 years of Polynesian navigation from Sāmoa, Tonga, and the Cook Islands. Tahiti became French protectorate (1842) and colony (1880); contemporary French Polynesia contains the Mururoa nuclear test sites (193 tests 1966-96, ongoing health effects).

sources.citation: Hawkesworth, J. (1773). An Account of the Voyages Undertaken... (Vol. 1 covers Wallis).
Salmond, A. (2009). Aphrodite's Island: The European Discovery of Tahiti. Viking.

location_name: Krakatau (Krakatoa), Sunda Strait, Dutch East Indies
main_actor: Krakatau volcano (VEI-6)
description: Climactic VEI-6 eruption of Krakatau on 26-27 August 1883. Four massive explosions on 27 August; the last (10:02 local) heard 4,800 km away on Rodrigues, audible across ~1/13 of Earth's surface. ~36,000 killed, mostly by tsunamis (up to 40 m height) along Java and Sumatra coasts. Stratospheric sulfate aerosols produced spectacular sunsets worldwide (inspiring Edvard Munch's 'The Scream'). Note: DB has 'krakatau' — likely without day precision; adding specific day.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'krakatau'. ETHICS: Dutch colonial authorities' response to indigenous casualties was limited; Javanese peasants received little reconstruction aid. The eruption's global climate and communications impact (first modern natural disaster reported worldwide by telegraph) reshaped Western understanding of volcanic hazards.

sources.citation: Winchester, S. (2003). Krakatoa: The Day the World Exploded. HarperCollins.

name_original: Bikini Atoll — Castle Bravo thermonuclear test
location_name: Bikini Atoll, Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (Marshall Islands)
main_actor: US Atomic Energy Commission; Lewis Strauss (chair); Marshallese communities (evacuated/contaminated)
description: Castle Bravo, the first US thermonuclear test, detonated on 1 March 1954 at Bikini Atoll. Expected yield 6 Mt; actual yield 15 Mt (largest US test ever). Fallout contaminated 11,000 km2 of the Pacific, heavily exposing inhabitants of Rongelap and Utirik atolls (70-75 km east), and the Japanese fishing boat Daigo Fukuryū Maru (Lucky Dragon No. 5) with one crewman dying. Marshallese exposed communities have experienced elevated cancer rates for generations.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Castle Bravo exposure of Marshallese is now classified as 'human radiation experiment' (US Department of Energy retrospective acknowledgment 1994). 167 Bikini residents were displaced beginning in 1946 with the promise of return; the atoll remains uninhabitable. The 1954 contamination radicalized the global anti-nuclear movement and inspired Godzilla (released November 1954).

sources.citation: Johnston, B. R. & Barker, H. M. (2008). Consequential Damages of Nuclear War: The Rongelap Report. Left Coast.

main_actor: Ian Smith (Prime Minister of Rhodesia); Rhodesian Front party; African National Congress (ZAPU, ZANU opponents)
description: The white-minority Rhodesian government under Ian Smith issued a Unilateral Declaration of Independence from the United Kingdom on 11 November 1965 — Armistice Day, chosen for symbolic weight. Rejected by UK and UN (Security Council Resolution 232, December 1966 — first UN economic sanctions ever). Bush war with ZANLA and ZIPRA guerrillas (1964-79) killed ~20,000-30,000. Lancaster House Agreement (December 1979) led to universal-suffrage elections and independence as Zimbabwe (18 April 1980).
ethical_notes: ETHICS: UDI's white-minority rationale echoed 1776 American Declaration rhetoric while explicitly excluding ~95% of Rhodesia's population (Black Africans) from political rights. The Rhodesian Front regime used counterinsurgency tactics including chemical weapons (CS gas, parathion-contaminated food/clothing) against civilians. Modern Zimbabwean politics continues to be shaped by the war's unresolved legacies (land reform, ZANU-PF's liberation credentials).

sources.citation: Meredith, M. (2002). Our Votes, Our Guns: Robert Mugabe and the Tragedy of Zimbabwe. PublicAffairs.

main_actor: Secretary of State George C. Marshall; Harvard University commencement
description: Secretary of State George C. Marshall delivered the Harvard commencement address on 5 June 1947, outlining what became the European Recovery Program ($13.3 billion 1948-51). Transformed postwar Europe's trajectory: reindustrialization, NATO foundation (1949), European integration (Schuman declaration 1950). Soviet bloc was offered participation but Stalin refused and blocked East European acceptance, hardening Cold War division. Note: DB has 'marshall plan / european recovery program' — adding specific speech date.
ethical_notes: SKIP candidate — DB has 'marshall plan / european recovery program' with likely YEAR precision. ETHICS: Marshall Plan aid was conditional on market liberalization and anticommunist politics — US attempts to shape European postwar political economy, successful in France and Italy (Communist parties excluded from government 1947) but less so in Britain (Labour's NHS 1948). Aid was racially selective: Black African colonies of participating European states received little.

sources.citation: Hogan, M. J. (1987). The Marshall Plan. Cambridge UP.

name_original: UN General Assembly adopts Universal Declaration of Human Rights
location_name: Palais de Chaillot, Paris (3rd UN GA session)
main_actor: Eleanor Roosevelt (committee chair); René Cassin (French drafter); P. C. Chang (Chinese); Charles Malik (Lebanese); Hansa Mehta (Indian)
description: UN General Assembly Resolution 217A adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights on 10 December 1948 at the Palais de Chaillot, Paris. 48 in favor, 0 against, 8 abstentions (Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Soviet bloc). The 30-article declaration set the foundation for modern international human rights law. 10 December is Human Rights Day.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the Declaration's universalist framing was shaped by non-Western drafters (Chang, Malik, Mehta) as well as Western ones, complicating later 'Asian values' and cultural-relativism critiques. Hansa Mehta of India insisted on replacing 'all men' with 'all human beings' in Article 1. The Declaration was not binding; binding treaties (ICCPR, ICESCR) came in 1966. Enforcement remains uneven.

sources.citation: Glendon, M. A. (2001). A World Made New: Eleanor Roosevelt and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. Random House.

main_actor: Japan Trench megathrust (Mw 9.1); TEPCO (Fukushima Daiichi nuclear plant)
description: Magnitude 9.1 earthquake off the Tōhoku coast on 11 March 2011 at 14:46 JST. Tsunami waves up to 40 m struck the coast, killing ~19,500 people. Inundation caused a Level 7 nuclear accident at TEPCO's Fukushima Daiichi plant (only second in history after Chornobyl). ~164,000 people displaced from the 20-km exclusion zone. Economic damage ~$235 billion — the costliest natural disaster in history.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Fukushima exposed TEPCO's and Japanese regulators' systemic neglect of tsunami risk despite geological warnings. The official Japanese parliamentary investigation (2012) called the disaster 'profoundly man-made'. ~1,700 excess deaths from evacuation-related stress occurred, concentrated among the elderly. Ongoing radioactive water releases (2023 - ) remain politically contested.

sources.citation: Kingston, J. ed. (2012). Natural Disaster and Nuclear Crisis in Japan. Routledge.

main_actor: Nojima Fault (Mw 6.9); Kobe metropolitan population (~1.5 million)
description: M6.9 earthquake struck Kobe at 05:46 JST on 17 January 1995. ~6,434 killed, ~43,000 injured; ~200,000 buildings destroyed. Revealed severe weaknesses in elevated highway design, older wooden housing, and disaster response coordination. Yakuza organizations (notably Yamaguchi-gumi) famously delivered aid faster than government. Triggered comprehensive seismic code overhauls and decentralization of emergency management.
ethical_notes: Zainichi Koreans and other marginalized residents (disproportionately in collapse-prone older housing) faced discrimination in aid distribution. The government's slow response discredited Prime Minister Murayama's Socialist-led coalition.

sources.citation: Edgington, D. W. (2010). Reconstructing Kobe. UBC Press.

name_original: First powered flight — Wright Brothers at Kitty Hawk
location_name: Kill Devil Hills, Kitty Hawk, Outer Banks, North Carolina
main_actor: Orville Wright (pilot on first flight); Wilbur Wright
description: First sustained, controlled, powered flight of a heavier-than-air aircraft by the Wright Flyer on 17 December 1903 at Kitty Hawk. Orville piloted the first flight (120 ft, 12 seconds, 10:35 local); four flights total, the longest 852 ft / 59 seconds by Wilbur. Opened the age of aviation.
ethical_notes: Alberto Santos-Dumont's 1906 Paris flight of the 14-bis is sometimes claimed as the 'first' public flight given the Wright Brothers' initial secrecy. French, Brazilian, and US national pride continue to contest aviation firsts.

sources.citation: McCullough, D. (2015). The Wright Brothers. Simon & Schuster.

location_name: St Mary's Hospital laboratory, Paddington, London
main_actor: Alexander Fleming (St Mary's Hospital Medical School); Ernst Chain, Howard Florey (later purification, Oxford 1940-41)
description: Alexander Fleming noticed on 28 September 1928 (traditional date) that a contaminating Penicillium notatum mould on a Staphylococcus culture plate had created a bacteria-free halo. Fleming published his observation in 1929 but did not develop clinical use. Chain and Florey at Oxford (1939-41) purified penicillin for therapeutic use, first given to a human patient 12 February 1941. Mass production (Peoria Lab 1941-44) saved countless Allied soldiers in WWII. Fleming, Chain, Florey shared the 1945 Nobel Prize in Medicine.
ethical_notes: Antibiotic resistance (first observed in the 1940s by Fleming himself) now kills ~1.3 million people per year globally. The mass-production history often omits the crucial role of women 'penicillin girls' in Peoria and the earlier observations of antibacterial moulds by Ernest Duchesne (1896) and others.

sources.citation: Bud, R. (2007). Penicillin: Triumph and Tragedy. Oxford UP.

location_name: Baikonur Cosmodrome, Kazakh SSR (launch); landing in Saratov Oblast, Russian SFSR
main_actor: Yuri Alekseyevich Gagarin; Chief Designer Sergei Korolev; Soviet space program
description: Yuri Gagarin completed the first human orbital spaceflight aboard Vostok 1 on 12 April 1961 — 108-minute single orbit of Earth. Launched 06:07 UTC from Baikonur; ejected from capsule at ~7 km altitude and parachuted to ground near Engels, Saratov Oblast, at 07:55 UTC. Gagarin became a global celebrity. 12 April is International Day of Human Space Flight.
ethical_notes: Gagarin's flight was initially shrouded in secrecy; the ejection landing (rather than capsule landing) was concealed for decades to claim compliance with FAI records requiring pilot-in-vehicle landing. Gagarin died in a 1968 MiG-15 training crash; cause remained classified until 2013.

sources.citation: Doran, J. & Bizony, P. (2011). Starman: The Truth Behind the Legend of Yuri Gagarin. Bloomsbury.

main_actor: Pope John XXIII; ~2,600 bishops; observers from non-Catholic Christian churches
description: Second Vatican Council opened by Pope John XXIII at St Peter's on 11 October 1962. Four sessions through 8 December 1965 under John XXIII and his successor Paul VI. Produced four constitutions, nine decrees, three declarations — most significantly on liturgy (Sacrosanctum Concilium, vernacular Mass), the Church (Lumen Gentium), the Church in the modern world (Gaudium et Spes), and religious freedom (Dignitatis Humanae). Revolutionized Catholic self-understanding and ecumenical relations. Nostra Aetate (1965) renounced Church teaching that Jews collectively killed Christ.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Vatican II's reforms remain contested within Catholicism — traditionalist movements (SSPX, FSSPX) reject liturgical reforms and certain declarations. The Council's engagement with modernity did not extend to women's ordination or teaching on homosexuality. Nostra Aetate's repudiation of antisemitic teaching came only after the Holocaust forced reckoning; earlier critical voices (Jules Isaac) had been marginal.

sources.citation: O'Malley, J. W. (2008). What Happened at Vatican II. Harvard UP.

calendar_note: Proleptic Julian. Dated via the lunar eclipse of 20/21 September 331 BCE (Julian), which Plutarch and Arrian place 11 days before the battle.
location_name: Gaugamela plain (Tel Gomel) near Mosul, Achaemenid Satrapy of Assyria
main_actor: Alexander III of Macedon; Darius III Codomannus of the Achaemenid Empire
description: Decisive Macedonian victory over the Achaemenid Empire. Alexander's ~47,000 troops routed Darius III's much larger army (ancient sources inflate to 250,000; modern estimates 50,000-120,000). Darius fled and was murdered in July 330 BCE by Bessus, satrap of Bactria. End of the Achaemenid dynasty after 220 years.
ethical_notes: All primary narratives are Greek/Roman (Arrian, Diodorus, Curtius, Plutarch), centuries after the battle. No Persian source survives. 'Persian defeat' framing obscures that Achaemenid institutions (satrapies, roads, coinage) were absorbed almost wholesale by Alexander's successors.

sources.citation: Marsden, E. W. (1964). The Campaign of Gaugamela. Liverpool UP.
Arrian, Anabasis III.7-15 (2nd c. CE).

calendar_note: Chinese sources place the battle in the seventh month of Tianbao 10; Arabic (al-Tabari) in Dhu'l-Hijjah 133 AH. Both correspond to July-August 751 CE Julian. Exact day not recorded.
location_name: Talas River valley (modern Taraz region), Karluk territory
main_actor: Abbasid Caliphate (Ziyad ibn Salih) with Karluk allies; Tang Dynasty (general Gao Xianzhi / Ko Son-ji, a Korean)
description: Abbasid-Tang clash on the Talas River. The Karluks, initially Tang allies, defected mid-battle, leading to the Tang defeat. Conventionally marks the western limit of Tang expansion and the eastern limit of Abbasid expansion. The traditional claim that papermaking technology was transmitted west via captured Chinese artisans is now considered plausible but not independently attested.
ethical_notes: General Gao Xianzhi, often described in Chinese sources as 'Tang', was ethnically Korean from Goguryeo — his career illustrates the multiethnic Tang military elite.

sources.citation: Beckwith, C. I. (1987). The Tibetan Empire in Central Asia. Princeton UP.
al-Tabari, Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk.
Zizhi Tongjian 資治通鑑 vol. 216 (1084 CE).

calendar_note: Julian calendar. The Milan meeting between Constantine and Licinius was February 313; the operative text is Licinius's rescript published at Nicomedia on 13 June 313, preserved by Lactantius.
main_actor: Emperor Constantine I; Emperor Licinius
description: Publication of the Licinian rescript extending full religious tolerance to Christians in the eastern Empire, mirroring Constantine's policy in the west. Property confiscated during the Diocletianic persecution ordered restored. Preserved verbatim in Lactantius's De mortibus persecutorum 48. NB: the existing DB entry 'edictum mediolanense' lacks day-precision.
ethical_notes: Modern scholarship (Barnes, Potter) stresses that no single 'Edict of Milan' document exists — the Milan meeting was a political summit; the operative legal text is the Licinius rescript. Toleration of paganism was preserved; persecution reversed direction only after Theodosius.

sources.citation: Lactantius, De mortibus persecutorum 48 (c. 314-315 CE).
Barnes, T. D. (1981). Constantine and Eusebius. Harvard UP.

location_name: Mount Tambora, Sumbawa, Sultanate of Bima (Dutch East Indies)
main_actor: Mount Tambora (VEI-7 stratovolcano)
description: Climactic paroxysm of the largest observed volcanic eruption in recorded history, beginning the evening of 10 April 1815 and peaking the following day. ~38 km3 (DRE) of magma ejected. Pyroclastic flows and tsunamis killed ~71,000 on Sumbawa and Lombok. Stratospheric sulfate veil triggered the 'Year Without a Summer' (1816) — crop failures and famine in New England, northern Europe, and Yunnan. Note: existing DB entry 'tambora 1815' lacks day-precision.
ethical_notes: The Tambora and Pekat languages and their speakers were effectively annihilated. Dutch colonial records undercount indigenous casualties. Climate effects fell hardest on subsistence farmers globally — Yunnan famine mortality alone may have exceeded eruption-zone deaths.

sources.citation: Oppenheimer, C. (2003). Progress in Physical Geography 27(2).
Wood, G. D. (2014). Tambora: The Eruption That Changed the World. Princeton UP.

location_name: Lakagígar fissure, south Iceland (Kingdom of Denmark-Norway)
main_actor: Laki (Lakagígar) fissure system
description: Fissure eruption beginning 8 June 1783 and continuing for eight months. ~14 km3 of basalt lava and vast SO2 releases. The 'Móðuharðindin' (Mist Hardships) killed ~20% of Iceland's population (~10,000) and halved livestock. Sulfate aerosols caused poor harvests across Europe and North Africa; several historians link Laki's climatic effects to France's 1787-88 harvest failures and the social preconditions of the French Revolution.
ethical_notes: Iceland's demographic catastrophe was compounded by the Danish trade monopoly (Einokun) restricting emergency grain imports. Reverend Jón Steingrímsson's 'Fire Sermon' (20 July 1783) is a unique eyewitness primary source.

sources.citation: Steingrímsson, J. (1788). Um jarðeldinn í Skaftafellssýslu.
Thordarson, T. & Self, S. (2003). J. Geophysical Research 108.

name_original: Diet of Worms — Luther refuses to recant
calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 28 April 1521.
location_name: Episcopal palace, Worms, Holy Roman Empire
main_actor: Martin Luther; Emperor Charles V; Johann Eck (interrogator)
description: On 17-18 April 1521, Martin Luther appeared before the Imperial Diet at Worms under imperial safe-conduct and refused to recant his writings. The phrase 'Hier stehe ich, ich kann nicht anders' (attested in the printed Acta, probably a later embellishment) became emblematic. The Edict of Worms (25 May) declared Luther an outlaw. Frederick the Wise engineered his protective 'abduction' to the Wartburg.
ethical_notes: Luther's theological courage is celebrated, but his late writings (Von den Juden und ihren Lügen, 1543) endorsed violent antisemitism and shaped centuries of persecution in Lutheran lands. Nazi propagandists explicitly cited Luther as authority.

sources.citation: Brecht, M. (1985-1993). Martin Luther (3 vols). Fortress Press.
Acta et res gestae D. Martini Lutheri in Comitiis Wormaciae (1521).

calendar_note: Julian calendar. Traditional posting date 31 October 1517; Iserloh (1968) argued Luther mailed the Latin theses to Archbishop Albrecht of Mainz rather than nailing them to the Schlosskirche door — modern scholars are divided.
location_name: Wittenberg, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire
main_actor: Martin Luther
description: Luther disseminated his 'Disputatio pro declaratione virtutis indulgentiarum' (95 Theses) on 31 October 1517, protesting the sale of indulgences by Johann Tetzel for the rebuilding of St Peter's. Within weeks, translated and printed, the theses circulated across Germany. Conventional start date of the Protestant Reformation.
ethical_notes: The theses were originally a Latin academic disputation, not a popular manifesto. The explosive reception owed to Tetzel's blatant commercialization and to the new technology of the printing press.

sources.citation: Luther, M. (1517). Disputatio pro declaratione virtutis indulgentiarum.
Roper, L. (2016). Martin Luther: Renegade and Prophet. Bodley Head.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 25 January 1559. Date reportedly chosen by John Dee as astrologically auspicious.
main_actor: Queen Elizabeth I; Owen Oglethorpe, Bishop of Carlisle (officiant)
description: Elizabeth Tudor crowned at Westminster Abbey after the death of her Catholic half-sister Mary I (17 November 1558). Most bishops refused to crown Elizabeth over religious dispute; only Oglethorpe agreed, under protest. The coronation inaugurated the decisive Protestant turn of the English Reformation. Elizabeth reigned 44 years until 1603.
ethical_notes: The 'Elizabethan Golden Age' is partly a Whig construction. Her reign also saw: penal laws against recusant Catholics, the Tudor plantation of Ireland, English slave-trading voyages (Hawkins from 1562), and harsh Poor Law regimes.

sources.citation: Starkey, D. (2001). Elizabeth: The Struggle for the Throne. HarperCollins.

calendar_note: Julian calendar; proleptic Gregorian 2 June 1618.
location_name: Prague Castle (Bohemian Chancellery), Kingdom of Bohemia
main_actor: Protestant Bohemian estates under Count Heinrich Matthias von Thurn; Catholic imperial regents Wilhelm Slavata and Jaroslav Bořita of Martinice (defenestrated, survived)
description: Protestant Bohemian nobles threw two Catholic imperial regents and their secretary Philip Fabricius from a window of Prague Castle. All three survived the ~21-metre fall (Catholic sources attributed it to divine intervention; Protestant sources to a dung heap). Triggered the Bohemian Revolt and the Thirty Years' War, which killed ~8 million people.
ethical_notes: The defenestration consciously echoed the First Defenestration of 1419, which began the Hussite Wars. Czech national historiography reads both as acts of religious liberty; imperial Catholic historiography as sedition.

sources.citation: Wilson, P. H. (2009). Europe's Tragedy: A History of the Thirty Years War. Allen Lane.

calendar_note: Gregorian calendar. In the French Revolutionary calendar adopted later that year: 2 Pluviôse An I.
location_name: Place de la Révolution (now Place de la Concorde), Paris, French Republic
main_actor: King Louis XVI; Charles-Henri Sanson (executioner); National Convention
description: Louis XVI was guillotined at 10:22 on 21 January 1793 after being convicted of treason (387 to 334 for execution) by the National Convention. His death inaugurated the French First Republic as a regicidal polity and triggered the War of the First Coalition. Marie Antoinette was executed 16 October 1793.
ethical_notes: The narrow execution vote revealed deep divisions. European monarchs closed ranks against revolution; royalist resistance in the Vendée intensified and was met by mass reprisal (estimated 170,000 killed 1793-96).

sources.citation: Hardman, J. (2016). The Life of Louis XVI. Yale UP.

location_name: Waterloo, United Kingdom of the Netherlands (modern Belgium)
main_actor: Duke of Wellington (Anglo-allied); Gebhard von Blücher (Prussian); Napoleon Bonaparte
description: Napoleon's final defeat. ~72,000 French engaged ~68,000 Anglo-allied troops under Wellington, reinforced in the afternoon by ~50,000 Prussians under Blücher. ~47,000 casualties in ~10 hours. Napoleon abdicated 22 June and was exiled to Saint Helena. Ended the Hundred Days and the Napoleonic Wars; inaugurated the Concert of Europe and roughly four decades of major-power peace in Europe.
ethical_notes: British memory centers Wellington; Prussian memory Blücher; French memory frames Waterloo as heroic tragedy. All three elide the ~200,000 Belgian and Dutch civilians displaced and the enslaved West Africans who fought (and died) in French colonial units during the Hundred Days.

sources.citation: Roberts, A. (2005). Waterloo. HarperCollins.

name_original: Battle of Austerlitz (Battle of the Three Emperors)
location_name: Austerlitz (Slavkov u Brna), Moravia, Austrian Empire
main_actor: Emperor Napoleon I; Emperor Francis II of Austria; Tsar Alexander I of Russia
description: Tactical masterpiece on the first anniversary of Napoleon's coronation. ~65,000 French defeated ~85,000 Austro-Russians; ~27,000 Allied casualties vs ~9,000 French. Led to the Treaty of Pressburg (26 December 1805), dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire (1806), and establishment of the Confederation of the Rhine.
ethical_notes: The dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire a year later ended an 844-year polity (962-1806) and created the territorial and constitutional vacuum out of which the 19th-century German national question grew.

sources.citation: Chandler, D. G. (1966). The Campaigns of Napoleon. Macmillan.

main_actor: Metternich (Austria); Castlereagh (Britain); Hardenberg (Prussia); Alexander I (Russia); Talleyrand (France)
description: Final Act of the Congress of Vienna (9 June 1815, nine days before Waterloo) — 216 articles reorganizing Europe after Napoleon. Created the German Confederation (39 states), partitioned Poland again, united the Dutch Netherlands with Belgium, joined Norway to Sweden. Established the 'Concert of Europe' intervention mechanism against revolution.
ethical_notes: The Congress restored dynastic legitimacy at the expense of national self-determination. Poles, Italians, Belgians, and Germans lived under imposed arrangements whose collapse defined 1830, 1848, 1859-71, and 1918.

sources.citation: Zamoyski, A. (2007). Rites of Peace. HarperPress.

main_actor: Napoleon Bonaparte; Pope Pius VII; Empress Josephine
description: Napoleon I crowned Emperor of the French at Notre-Dame. Pope Pius VII attended but Napoleon placed the crown on his own head, signaling that his authority was not derived from the Church. Ended the French Consulate and established the Premier Empire. Commemorated by Jacques-Louis David's painting Le Sacre (1807).
ethical_notes: The staging combined Carolingian symbolism (the Pope) with revolutionary self-assertion (the self-crowning), consciously bypassing the Bourbon Rheims tradition.

sources.citation: Englund, S. (2004). Napoleon: A Political Life. Scribner.

main_actor: Second Continental Congress; Thomas Jefferson (principal author); John Hancock (Congress President)
description: The Second Continental Congress adopted the Declaration of Independence on 4 July 1776. Independence was voted 2 July; the text was adopted 4 July; most delegates signed the engrossed parchment 2 August. Founding text of the United States.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: the 'all men are created equal' clause excluded enslaved Black people, Indigenous peoples, and women. Jefferson himself enslaved over 600 people across his lifetime. The Declaration's grievance list called Indigenous peoples 'merciless Indian Savages', a rhetorical foundation of dispossession.

sources.citation: Maier, P. (1997). American Scripture. Knopf.

name_original: Signing of the United States Constitution
main_actor: Constitutional Convention; George Washington (presiding); James Madison (architect)
description: 39 of 55 delegates signed the US Constitution on 17 September 1787 after a four-month convention. Replaced the Articles of Confederation. Ratified by the ninth state (New Hampshire) 21 June 1788; effective 4 March 1789. Commemorated as Constitution Day / Citizenship Day.
ethical_notes: The Constitution codified the Three-Fifths Compromise (Article I §2) and guaranteed the Atlantic slave trade until 1808 (Article I §9). Indigenous peoples were excluded from constitutional citizenship until the Indian Citizenship Act of 1924.

sources.citation: Beeman, R. (2009). Plain, Honest Men. Random House.

name_original: Slavery Abolition Act takes effect — British Empire
location_name: British Empire (legislated in Westminster, applied throughout colonies)
main_actor: UK Parliament; enslaved peoples of the Caribbean, Mauritius, and Cape Colony; Baptist War insurgents (Jamaica 1831-32)
description: The Slavery Abolition Act 1833 took effect 1 August 1834. ~800,000 enslaved people across the British Empire were reclassified as 'apprentices' (forced unpaid work for former owners for 4-6 years; apprenticeship ended 1 August 1838). £20 million compensation was paid to slave-owners — ~40% of the UK Treasury's annual revenue — with no compensation to formerly enslaved people. East India Company territories were exempted until 1843.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: British 'abolition' narratives center Wilberforce and Parliament, eliding the Caribbean revolts (Haitian Revolution 1791, Bussa 1816 Barbados, Demerara 1823, Baptist War 1831-32 Jamaica) that made slavery economically untenable. The £20M slave-owner compensation loan was only paid off by UK taxpayers in 2015.

sources.citation: Drescher, S. (2009). Abolition. Cambridge UP.

main_actor: President Abraham Lincoln; enslaved African-Americans
description: Lincoln's Emancipation Proclamation, issued 22 September 1862, took effect 1 January 1863, declaring enslaved people in Confederate-held areas (~3.5 million people) free. It did not apply to Union-loyal border slave states (Delaware, Maryland, Kentucky, Missouri, West Virginia) — those ~400,000 enslaved people were freed by the Thirteenth Amendment (6 December 1865).
ethical_notes: Practical emancipation depended on Union military advance. Enslaved people in Texas learned of freedom only on 19 June 1865 (commemorated as Juneteenth, US federal holiday since 2021). Lincoln's earlier colonization proposals (removing Black Americans to Liberia or Central America) are often omitted from celebratory narratives.

sources.citation: Foner, E. (2010). The Fiery Trial. Norton.

main_actor: Gavrilo Princip (Young Bosnia); Archduke Franz Ferdinand and Sophie Chotek (killed)
description: On the morning of 28 June 1914, Gavrilo Princip, a 19-year-old Bosnian Serb of Young Bosnia, shot Archduke Franz Ferdinand, heir presumptive to Austria-Hungary, and his wife Sophie. The date was Vidovdan, the Serb national commemoration of the 1389 Battle of Kosovo. Triggered the July Crisis leading to World War I.
ethical_notes: The Austrian ultimatum (23 July 1914) reflected pre-existing war aims; the assassination provided occasion. The Black Hand (Apis, Serbian military intelligence) was implicated, though the Serbian government's role is contested. ~20 million people died in the ensuing war.

sources.citation: Dedijer, V. (1966). The Road to Sarajevo.
Clark, C. (2012). The Sleepwalkers.

main_actor: Friedrich Paulus (surrendered, 6th Army); Vasily Chuikov, Georgy Zhukov (Red Army)
description: Final surrender of the remnants of the German 6th Army at Stalingrad on 2 February 1943, ending the five-month battle. ~91,000 Germans captured — only ~5,000-6,000 survived Soviet captivity. Total battle casualties ~2 million (military and civilian). Strategic turning point of the Eastern Front.
ethical_notes: Stalin's 19 August 1942 refusal to evacuate civilians condemned ~500,000 Stalingraders. Both Wehrmacht and NKVD committed mass atrocities during and after the battle. Soviet propaganda framed the victory as vindication of the Stalinist order, obscuring the chaos of the 1941-42 collapse.

sources.citation: Beevor, A. (1998). Stalingrad. Viking.
